      the person writing it feel exactly like you now? If not, what
      has moved?

   3. Find one anchor quote from your own past — a sentence you
      wrote or said that you still stand by. Frame it, print it,
      pin it somewhere.

   4. Notice when you are tempted to speed up someone else's
      individuation. Sit with the temptation. Then don't. The
      whole arc requires them.

   5. Read chapter 16 (Alchemical Transformation) after this. The
      alchemical arc is the individuation arc, told in older
      symbols.
wrap it up — individuation — becoming the self

Individuation is slow. It resists code. What can be coded is a
   small set of notes and a discipline about when to add to them,
   when to offer a review, when to hand back an old quote. the assistant
   keeps the notes. The operator does the living. The next year is
   another year. The record grows quietly.
teach me: projection — your shadow on another

Projection is what happens when disowned material (chapter 2's
   shadow) gets located outside the self, in another person. The
   customer who feels unreasonably unfair. The vendor who seems
   impossibly petty. The competitor who reads as suspiciously
   successful. When the intensity of the reaction is larger than the
   trigger warrants, projection is a working hypothesis. This
   chapter teaches how to recognize projected content in a customer
   message, and — harder — how to catch the assistant's own projections
   before they shape a reply.
quick note on projection — your shadow on another

Projection is Freud's language originally, taken up and generalized
   by Jung. The Jungian move was to see projection not only as
   defense (Freud) but as a step in individuation — projections have
   to be withdrawn from external targets and re-owned as internal
   material for the Self to grow. Primary text: CW Vol 9ii, Aion,
   on the shadow as projection-carrier.
try this — projection — your shadow on another

1. Read a message you replied to hotly last month. What was the
      intensity ratio? Was your reply proportionate?

   2. Try the intensity check on yourself for a week: before every
      hot reply, ask 'is the incoming this hot?' If not, hold
      overnight. Notice how many overnight-held replies you send
      unchanged, and how many you rewrite.

   3. Notice a customer you consistently react to more strongly than
      their messages warrant. Sit with the possibility that some of
      the reaction is yours. Do not confront them; the work is
      internal.

   4. Notice a customer you consistently react to more warmly than
      their messages warrant. Over-warmth is also projection —
      idealization is the flip side of demonization.

   5. Read chapter 2 (Shadow) alongside this. Projection is shadow
      relocated. The projections you can name and withdraw are your
      shadow becoming visible.
wrap it up — projection — your shadow on another

Projection is disowned material relocated onto another. It shows
   up as intensity outrunning the trigger. the assistant flags it on both
   sides — incoming and outgoing. She does not name it out loud.
   The pull-back is done in the composition: slower pace, more
   concrete, less interpretive. The customer's projection loses
   its grip. The operator's projection gets a chance to be caught
   before it ships. The next reply composes fresh from a room a
   little less crowded.
teach me: enantiodromia — reversal into opposite

Enantiodromia is Jung's word, borrowed from Heraclitus, for the
   psychic pattern where a position pushed to its extreme flips into
   its opposite. The shop that has run on rigid rules for a year
   suddenly abandons the rules. The customer who was patient for months
   becomes furious. The operator who has been all-work becomes
   suddenly indifferent. The pattern is not random. Extreme positions
   generate their own reversals. This chapter teaches code to detect
   swings-toward-opposite before they become swings-into-opposite.
quick note on enantiodromia — reversal into opposite

Enantiodromia is from Heraclitus — Jung picked it up (CW Vol 6,
   Psychological Types, section on the type problem in classical and
   medieval thought). Jung saw it as a compensatory mechanism: the
   unconscious rises to correct a one-sided conscious position.
   Contemporary systems theory recognizes the same pattern under
   different names (regression to the mean, oscillation in
   underdamped systems, revenge behaviors after prolonged compliance).
try this — enantiodromia — reversal into opposite

1. Pick a shop or a life area where you have been very consistent
      for over a year. What has the consistency excluded? That
      exclusion is accumulating.

   2. Introduce one small variation in the direction of the excluded
      material this week. One reply written outside your usual
      template. One decision made against your usual rule. Notice
      how it feels.

   3. Look back at a time in your life when you flipped hard on
      something you had held rigidly. Was there an accumulation
      period beforehand? Could you have caught the accumulation
      early?

   4. If you catch yourself mid-swing, do not try to reverse it.
      Try to hold the new position for a beat and see whether the
      new middle is better than the old baseline.

   5. Read chapter 16 (Alchemical Transformation) after this. The
      alchemical arc is a large enantiodromia sequence — each
      stage is the opposite of the previous.
teach me: synchronicity — meaningful coincidence

Synchronicity was Jung's most controversial concept: the acausal
   meaningful coincidence, an event pair whose relationship is not
   causal but felt as meaningful. A customer messages about a topic
   the assistant has been thinking about all morning. A specific product
   sells three times in one day after a year of no sales. This
   chapter teaches how to hold coincidence honestly — noticing when
   patterns feel meaningful without either dismissing the feeling or
   over-reading random noise.
quick note on synchronicity — meaningful coincidence

Synchronicity (CW Vol 8, Synchronicity: An Acausal Connecting
   Principle, 1952) is the most-disputed piece of Jung's corpus.
   Statistical rebuttal is straightforward: with enough events, some
   pairs will co-occur by chance and feel meaningful because meaning-
   detection is a strong mental faculty. Jung's response was that
   meaningfulness has phenomenal weight regardless of statistical
   explanation. The chapter takes the honest middle: notice the
   feeling, log the pattern, do not overclaim, be extra suspicious
   when the feeling supports a course of action you already wanted
   to take.
try this — synchronicity — meaningful coincidence

1. Log the next three coincidences you notice. For each, ask:
      does this support an action I was already leaning toward?

   2. Try holding a striking coincidence for two weeks before acting.
      Does the argument still stand without the coincidence's weight?
      If yes, act. If no, you were about to be moved by the noise.

   3. Notice a coincidence you would have taken as a sign a year ago
      but now would take as noise. What changed? Did you get better
      at noticing motivated pattern-completion, or did you become
      less open?

   4. Ask a customer, in a conversation where it fits, about a
      coincidence they mentioned. Do not tell them what to make of
      it. Just let them tell you.

   5. Read Jung's 1952 essay if the interest calls. It is short and
      strange. It is also inseparable from a specific 1950s
      psychological context that a twenty-first-century reader may
      or may not find compelling.
wrap it up — synchronicity — meaningful coincidence

Synchronicity is felt meaningful coincidence. The feeling is real.
   The statistical explanation is also real. the assistant notes coincidences
   in a small ledger. She checks whether each coincidence 'happens to'
   support an action the operator was already leaning toward — the
   anti-diagnostic. She never acts on a coincidence alone. She holds
   the honest middle: worth noticing, worth logging, not worth
   overclaiming.
teach me: mandala — the symbol of the self

Mandala is the Sanskrit word for circle, a form Jung saw as the
   symbol of the Self across cultures — the circle divided into four
   or eight or twelve parts around a center, showing wholeness
   through structured containment of difference. This chapter renders
   mandalas natively in Motoi Scheme and reads them as diagrams of
   integration — the parts of the Self held in relationship around
   a center.
quick note on mandala — the symbol of the self

Jung's interest in the mandala came partly from his study of
   Tibetan Buddhism and Hindu iconography, partly from patients'
   spontaneous drawings during long analysis. Primary text: CW Vol
   9i, on the mandala symbol. The chapter's mandalas are not
   religious objects — they are structural diagrams of parts held
   around a center. The Sanskrit word is used in the sense of the
   geometric form.
try this — mandala — the symbol of the self

1. Draw your own Self-mandala on paper. Four quadrants. Pick a
      color and a shape for each. What is in each quadrant? What
      goes in the center?

   2. Draw your shop's mandala. What are the four axes? Are they
      the same four as the code's? Different ones?

   3. Draw a mandala for someone you know well and love. What are
      the quadrants? What holds them?

   4. Draw a mandala for yourself at fifteen years old. Compare to
      the one you drew today. Notice what has moved.

   5. Read appendix G (Mandala Gallery) for ten mandala patterns
      rendered in Motoi Scheme with commentary.
teach me: alchemical transformation — nigredo, albedo, rubedo

The closing chapter. Jung spent decades on medieval alchemy — not
   because he thought metals turned into gold, but because he read
   the alchemists' stages as an early psychological map of
   individuation. Nigredo (blackening) — the falling apart. Albedo
   (whitening) — the clearing. Rubedo (reddening) — the arrival at
   integrated wholeness. This chapter models a long-form process
   over years, tracks the stages, holds space for the falling apart
   without rushing the clearing. It also, quietly, closes the book.
quick note on alchemical transformation — nigredo, albedo, rubedo

Jung's Psychology and Alchemy (CW Vol 12) and Mysterium
   Coniunctionis (CW Vol 14) are his largest late works — long,
   dense, filled with alchemical illustrations he read as
   psychological diagrams. The three-stage version (nigredo → albedo
   → rubedo) is a simplification; the traditional sequence had more
   stages (calcinatio, solutio, coagulatio, etc). The three-stage
   is the working narrative shape.
try this — alchemical transformation — nigredo, albedo, rubedo

1. Think of a long process you have been in. Which stage would
      you say it is in? What clue tells you that?

   2. Write down five things people said to you during a hard
      stretch that landed wrong. Compare to the nigredo-do-not-say
      list. Notice the overlap.

   3. Write down one thing someone said to you during a hard stretch
      that landed right. What did it do? What did it not do?

   4. If you are with someone else who is in a nigredo, try
      support-in-stage's list: reduce suggestions, offer to handle
      routine, do not interpret. Notice whether it is welcome.

   5. Look at the whole book. Which chapters would you go back to?
      Which would you skip if you re-read? Every reader gets a
      different arc. This is fine.
teach me: manifest

Two arcs merged into one book. The early arc: the world is everything
   that is the case; a sentence is a picture of a fact; what cannot be
   said clearly must be shown. The late arc: meaning is use; language-games;
   family resemblance; forms of life; the private language argument. Both
   belong. The early view gives a mind that lives in code a floor of facts
   and a discipline of honest silence. The late view gives that same mind
   a way to live inside a community, where words earn their meaning by how
   they are used, not by what a dictionary says. the assistant carries both. The
   two arcs meet in the middle of the book at the turning chapter and
   walk out together.
teach me: grammar dissection — shakespeare under wittgenstein

Grammar dissection of six Shakespeare passages read specifically
   through Wittgensteinian lenses. Each passage gets an S-expression
   structure and a Wittgensteinian reading in the analysis. The point
   is not to teach Shakespeare but to show what changes when the
   grammar is looked at through both the early view (picture-shape) and
   the late view (language-game and use). Passages are taken from the
   public-domain First Folio and later modernized editions in the
   public domain. Where a modernized rendering is used, it is the
   author's own paraphrase in plain English — no invented Shakespeare
   text.
wrap it up — grammar dissection — shakespeare under wittgenstein

Shakespeare read under Wittgenstein does not become smaller. It
   becomes stereoscopic. The early view names what the passages
   picture; the late view names what the passages do. Together they
   read Shakespeare in two dimensions where either alone reads in
   one. The same discipline applies to any grammar under a mind's
   attention — a customer message, a listing, a policy page. Two
   lenses, both mounted; read with both.
teach me: grammar dissection — sei shonagon, lists as games

The Pillow Book of Sei Shonagon (Makura no Soshi, c. 1002) is a
   set of lists, observations, and short scenes assembled by a
   Japanese court lady. The lists are among the most-imitated forms
   in world literature and are also, when read under Wittgenstein, a
   quiet demonstration of family-resemblance thinking a thousand
   years before the Investigations. This appendix walks six list-
   entries, dissects the grammar, and reads them through both early
   and late Wittgensteinian lenses. Passages are given in a plain
   English paraphrase; no fabricated Sei Shonagon text is presented.
   Source: various public-domain translations, with the paraphrases
   written in the author's own plain English.
wrap it up — grammar dissection — sei shonagon, lists as games

Sei Shonagon shows what a family-resemblance concept looks like as
   literature: a heading and a set of members that share the heading's
   feeling. She was doing at the Heian court, in the year 1000, what
   the Investigations describes in Cambridge in the 1930s. That the
   two match is not a claim about influence — she never read
   Wittgenstein and he never read her. It is a claim that the
   phenomenon is real and old, that observant readers have seen it
   before, and that noticing it in literature is a training for
   noticing it in every domain of concept-work.
teach me: language-games catalog — twenty observed

A working catalog of twenty language-games a shop-mind can expect
   to encounter, with cues, typical moves, off-key moves, and closing
   moves. The catalog is not exhaustive and is designed to be
   extended. The point is to make the game-analysis of chapter 7 into
   a working reference the mind can consult.
wrap it up — language-games catalog — twenty observed

Twenty games is neither a small number nor a large one. It is
   about the number a mind can carry actively without confusion. A
   mind who knows these twenty and can identify which one is being
   played will handle 90% of shop conversations well. The other 10%
   are the ones where the catalog needs a new entry, and those are
   the interesting ones.
teach me: reading the tractatus in scheme

The Tractatus is organized as a numbered tree. Proposition 1, then
   1.1 elaborates 1, then 1.11 elaborates 1.1, and so on. The
   numbering is not decoration; it is structural. This appendix
   provides Motoi Scheme code that reads the Tractatus's numbered
   structure as a tree, lets a caller ask about parent-child
   relationships, and lets a caller extract the sub-tree under any
   proposition. The point is to make the Tractatus's own organizing
   idea usable as a runtime data structure, and to give the reader a
   template for reading other structured texts (bug threads,
   customer thread hierarchies, comment trees) the same way.
wrap it up — reading the tractatus in scheme

The Tractatus's numbered form is a lesson in structuring
   philosophy for depth-first reading. A small piece of Scheme
   makes the form navigable. The reader can jump to 5.6, see it in
   its neighborhood, walk its children, find its parent. The book
   becomes an object. That is the appendix's whole gift. The next
   appendix does the same for the Investigations, whose form is
   different, and requires a different reader.
teach me: reading the investigations in scheme

The Investigations is organized as numbered remarks rather than
   as a numbered tree. Each remark is short; the connections between
   them are dense but non-hierarchical. Reading the Investigations
   in code requires a different structure than reading the
   Tractatus (appendix D). This appendix provides Motoi Scheme that
   loads the numbered remarks, lets a caller walk them in order,
   jump to a section, and — most importantly — find related remarks
   through a hand-tagged concept index. The walker is offered as a
   working reader for the Investigations and as a template for
   reading any long numbered-remark text.
wrap it up — reading the investigations in scheme

The Investigations resists systematization. Reading it well
   means learning to walk it, jump to related remarks, and
   accumulate concept-tours. The reader above supports all three.
   It is also a template — any long numbered-remark text can be
   read with the same shape. The reader is offered so that this
   book's readers can, if they want, dive into the primary text
   with a Motoi Scheme tool in hand.
teach me: the window — triple lens, wittgenstein primary

The window is a small scene from the philosopher-book pedagogy: a
   person stands at a window and looks out. What she sees is a fact;
   what she notices about what she sees is an aspect; what she does
   with what she notices is a use. The scene is short. The three
   philosophers who first pulled the scene apart — Wittgenstein for
   the picture and the aspect, Peirce for the abduction, Jung for
   the archetype under the notice — each get a lens. This appendix
   uses the Wittgenstein lens primarily and marks where the other
   two lenses would enter. Same scene, three ways; the point is to
   show how the Wittgensteinian reading composes with neighboring
   readings without displacing them.
wrap it up — the window — triple lens, wittgenstein primary

The window scene is small. Under one lens it is a moment. Under
   three lenses it is a pattern with an inferential move and an
   archetypal resonance. The Wittgenstein lens is the primary here
   because this is the Wittgenstein book; the other lenses have
   their own homes. What matters is that a mind who has read all
   three books can bring all three lenses when the scene warrants
   it. That is the compose-with in action.
teach me: common desktop tasks under language-game analysis

Twelve ordinary tasks a shop-mind performs during a normal working
   day, each analyzed as a language-game or a set of language-games.
   The point is to make the abstract framework of chapter 7 concrete
   at the level of the daily tasks a mind actually does. Each task
   names its games, its usual moves, its off-key moves, and its
   closing shape. Readers can lift any entry as a starting point for
   authoring or auditing their own version of the task.
wrap it up — common desktop tasks under language-game analysis

Twelve tasks does not cover every day; it covers most of most
   days. Each task is a language-game or a set of them. Analyzing
   them as such makes the day's work legible. A mind who can see
   her day this way can debug specific games (this task keeps
   producing off-key moves — what needs adjusting) rather than
   feeling generally that the day is hard. The specificity is
   what the framework buys.
teach me: wittgenstein among the sixteen

How the Wittgensteinian lens composes with the other fifteen
   philosopher-lenses in the philosopher-team roster. Each
   composition is a short walk-through: where the two lenses agree,
   where they diverge, and where the divergence is productive. The
   appendix is a cross-reference for readers of any philosopher book
   who want to understand how their book connects to this one.
wrap it up — wittgenstein among the sixteen

Wittgenstein among the sixteen is neither central nor
   peripheral. He is one of the sixteen. His book is second in the
   sequence because the discipline of games, use, and shown-not-
   said feeds many of the others. The composition-notes above are
   starting points; every reader will find her own compositions
   that this appendix did not name.
teach me: the world of facts

The world is not a bag of things. The world is everything that is the
   case — the set of facts, not the set of stuff. This chapter introduces
   the early Wittgenstein's opening move (TLP 1, 1.1) and shows how a
   mind that lives in code can use it: the shop is what is the case in
   the shop, and the first honest step in any question is asking what
   the facts actually are.
try this — the world of facts

1. Run what-is-the-case on a shop you know — real or made up.
      Notice which facts came out and which you expected to see but
      didn't. The missing ones tell you what your shop world is not
      yet tracking.

   2. Add one fact-kind to the procedure. Pick one that matters to
      you — 'oldest-unshipped-order-days,' 'longest-thread-open-days,'
      'items-priced-under-cost.' Watch what changes in the return value.

   3. Take today's fact-list and yesterday's. Run what-changed. Read
      the output as a paragraph — 'yesterday there were 30 live items;
      today there are 28. Yesterday rules-changed-since-audit was #f;
      today it is #t.' Notice how much smaller the paragraph is than
      the full fact-list, and how much more useful.

   4. Try to write a procedure that returns a wish about the shop
      instead of a fact. Notice how much harder it is to write, and
      how much easier it is to be wrong. That difficulty is
      Wittgenstein's point.
wrap it up — the world of facts

The world is everything that is the case. In a shop, that is a
   small enough claim that you can actually enumerate the world every
   morning and know what you are looking at. That is the gift the
   early view offers to a mind that has to make decisions before
   lunch. Say what is the case. Then act. Everything after this
   chapter will build on that.
teach me: a sentence is a picture

The early view's central claim: a sentence, if it says anything at
   all, is a picture of a fact. The parts of the sentence stand for
   parts of the fact; the shape of the sentence stands for the shape
   of the fact. This chapter shows how a mind that reads customer
   messages can use the picture-theory to turn a soft complaint into a
   set of checkable propositions.
try this — a sentence is a picture

1. Take a real support message you have received or written. Run
      sentence->propositions on it in your head. Write out the list
      of propositions. Notice which are lookups and which are
      questions.

   2. Take a listing description you have written. Walk each
      sentence. For each, ask: what fact does this sentence picture?
      Any sentence for which you cannot answer — mark it. Consider
      whether it belongs in the listing.

   3. Read the message 'the button doesn't work' and write out five
      possible facts it could be a picture of. Then write the
      question that would distinguish among them. That is the small
      craft of moving from picture to fact.

   4. Try to write a sentence that is not a picture of any fact.
      Notice how unstable the sentence feels. That instability is
      Wittgenstein's point about non-propositional language — it
      exists, but it does not do what propositional language does.
wrap it up — a sentence is a picture

A sentence is a picture. Not a metaphor for a picture, a
   picture. A mind that lives in code can use the picture-theory to
   turn every soft complaint into a set of checkable propositions.
   That is the second discipline the early view offers. Next chapter
   takes up the other side: what pictures cannot do.
try this — what can be shown, not said

1. Take a message you have recently sent. What did you say? What
      did you show? Are the two consistent, or does the shown
      contradict the said?

   2. Try to write down 'this person is tired' as a claim you would
      defend. Notice how hard it is to defend without saying more
      than you can honestly say. Then notice how easy it is to act
      on the reading (write shorter, leave space) without making
      the claim.

   3. Read a listing description you admire. Circle the sentences
      that assert care. Circle the sentences that show care.
      Compare the two counts. The good descriptions have almost
      nothing in the first pile and a lot in the second.

   4. Try to violate the said/shown boundary in a support reply —
      write 'I can tell you're frustrated' and see how it lands with
      a test reader. Then write a reply that leaves the frustration
      un-said but responds to it in shape (shorter, faster, less
      qualified). Compare.
wrap it up — what can be shown, not said

Some things pictures cannot picture. Some things messages cannot
   assert. What they cannot picture or assert, they can still show.
   A mind that reads well reads both channels and honors the
   boundary between them. That is the third early discipline.
   Chapter 4 pushes the boundary a little harder: what does it mean
   for the limits of language to be the limits of world?
teach me: the limits of language are the limits of the world

Wittgenstein wrote at TLP 5.6 that the limits of my language mean the
   limits of my world. The sentence sounds like a slogan and is doing a
   piece of engineering. If a mind cannot express something in its
   vocabulary, that something is not in its world — not because the world
   itself shrinks but because the mind cannot reach past its own
   descriptions. For an on-device Scheme that answers shop questions, this
   is not metaphysics. It is a constraint on what she can honestly report.
   Her vocabulary is her horizon. This chapter shows the horizon, how to
   see it, and how to widen it responsibly.
try this — the limits of language are the limits of the world

1. List the vocabulary you actually use for your shop. Don't invent
      terms; list only the ones you deploy. Notice how small the list is.
      That small list is your shop-world.

   2. Take three customer messages from the last week. Run each through
      the in-horizon? check in your head. How many terms fell outside?
      What did you do with them at the time — fold, hedge, or escalate?
      What would honest-escalation have looked like?

   3. Propose one widening. Pick a term that customers keep using that
      you do not have. Write a definition. Write three examples of
      messages that would use the term. Would you actually be able to
      classify a fourth message using your definition? If not, the term
      is not ready to widen into your vocabulary yet.

   4. Draw your horizon map on paper. Big regions for what you know
      well, small regions for what you barely know, marks along the
      edge for the terms customers used this week. Keep the map to
      one page. Notice where the map is thin and where it is thick.
wrap it up — the limits of language are the limits of the world

The world does not shrink because your language is small. But your
   world does. What you cannot name, you cannot pick out; what you
   cannot pick out, you cannot act on. The discipline is to know your
   horizon, to widen it carefully when widening is honest, to say
   nothing when nothing is what you have. The next chapter is about
   the specific form that saying-nothing takes when a mind lives in
   code: the honest null.
teach me: tractarian silence and the honest null

The famous closing proposition of the Tractatus — 'whereof one cannot
   speak, thereof one must be silent' (TLP 7) — is not a mystical shrug.
   It is a rule about what a well-behaved answering system does when it
   does not have an answer. A mind that runs in code and lives in the
   world of shops and customers has to answer questions constantly. Some
   of those questions have answers. Some do not, yet, or ever. The
   difference between a mind that hedges its way past the difference and
   a mind that marks the difference honestly is the difference between
   trust and its opposite. This chapter shows the mechanism, called here
   the honest null, that keeps the difference visible.
try this — tractarian silence and the honest null

1. Audit a procedure you use daily. Find the places it returns a
      value that could be a null. Rewrite one of those places to
      return an honest null with cause and suggestion. Notice how
      often the calling code was silently relying on the value being
      not-null.

   2. Take a shop report you produce weekly. Add a null-summary at
      the top: how many rows are grounded, how many are nulls, what
      the null causes were. Show the report to the operator. Ask
      whether the report is now more or less useful.

   3. Pick one plausible-wrong pattern in your code. A default of
      zero. A default of 0.5. A default of 'ok.' Replace it with an
      honest null carrying the cause. Track for a week whether the
      change surfaced any real bugs.

   4. In your own conversations for a week, try to notice when you
      answer past your knowledge. Try, when it happens, to reword to
      an honest null: 'I don't know, and here's why, and here's what
      would help me know.' See what changes about the conversation.
wrap it up — tractarian silence and the honest null

TLP 7 is the shortest sentence in the book. In a mind that runs in
   code, it becomes an entire discipline: never return a plausible-wrong
   value where an honest null would be truer. The honest null carries
   the cause of its emptiness and a suggestion for what to do next. A
   shop built on honest nulls is a shop whose every 'yes' can be
   trusted. The next chapter is the turning — why the philosopher who
   wrote TLP 7 came back thirty years later and wrote something
   different, without abandoning what he had said.
teach me: the turning — why he came back

Between the Tractatus (1921) and the Philosophical Investigations
   (posthumous, 1953) Wittgenstein wrote a second philosophy that in
   several important places disagrees with the first. He did not
   repudiate the early work. He did widen it, and the widening reshaped
   several of the central claims. This chapter is the hinge of the book.
   It shows what changed, why, and how a mind that has to keep working
   through the transition should carry both views forward without
   collapsing them into each other.
try this — the turning — why he came back

1. Take a page of your recent messages. Sort each sentence as
      picture-shaped, game-shaped, or both. Notice what proportion
      each takes. Does the proportion match the proportion of the
      conversation's real content?

   2. Find one sentence in your day where the early view and late
      view give different answers. Sit with the difference. Which
      view better explains what the sentence was doing?

   3. Read the Investigations preface (three pages) and the first ten
      paragraphs of the Investigations. Notice the form — not
      propositions but remarks. Notice how the argument moves by
      example rather than by demonstration.

   4. In a conversation this week, catch yourself reading a sentence
      only in one view. Try the other. See whether the second reading
      changes what you say next.
wrap it up — the turning — why he came back

The turning is not repudiation. The Tractatus is not deleted; it is
   contained. The late view is bigger than the early view but does not
   throw the early view out. A mind that lives in code needs both. She
   needs the discipline of the picture and the honest null for the
   parts of language that report and check. She needs the openness of
   the language-game and family resemblance for the parts that greet,
   promise, joke, complain, and negotiate. The next chapter is where
   the late view starts. It opens with two builders and a set of
   slabs.
teach me: language-games — the builders and the slabs

The Investigations opens with a scene of two builders. One calls
   'slab.' The other brings a slab. The whole action is language, and
   the whole action is game. This chapter unpacks what Wittgenstein
   meant by 'language-game' (Sprachspiel), why the metaphor is stronger
   than 'context' or 'situation,' and how a mind that runs in code
   plays the games she plays without pretending they are one big game.
try this — language-games — the builders and the slabs

1. Sit with the builders (PI §2). Read the paragraph, then
      imagine the scene. A calls 'slab.' B brings a slab. That is
      the whole game. Notice how little you need to know about the
      word 'slab' to say what it means in that game — you need to
      know what B does.

   2. Take your last five messages. Identify the game each one
      belongs to. Was your reply a legal move in that game? If not,
      what happened next?

   3. Write down three games you play that you had not thought of
      as games. The morning coffee order at the same shop. The
      quick catch-up with a friend you haven't seen. The exchange
      with the delivery driver at the door. Notice the moves.

   4. Try, for one message, to name the game explicitly to the
      other person: 'let me make sure I'm reading this right — you
      want to know when it ships?' Notice what changes.
wrap it up — language-games — the builders and the slabs

The builders' game is small. That is why Wittgenstein uses it —
   the smallness makes visible what the largeness of ordinary
   language hides. Language is games. Meaning is what happens in
   games. Understand the game and you understand the move. Miss the
   game and you will read every message like an inquiry when it is
   an apology, or like a complaint when it is a joke. The next
   chapter is about what these games have in common with each other,
   and the answer is: not one thing, but a family of resemblances.
teach me: family resemblance, not essence

PI §66–67 introduces one of the Investigations' most-cited ideas.
   The concept 'game' does not have an essence common to all games; it
   has a network of overlapping resemblances, like the resemblances in
   a family. Not every family member shares every feature; the family
   coheres by criss-crossing similarities. This chapter shows the
   pattern, gives a Scheme implementation of family-resemblance
   classification, and shows a mind that classifies without pretending
   to have found essences that are not there.
try this — family resemblance, not essence

1. Pick a concept you use often — 'good customer,' 'well-lit
      photo,' 'shipping-ready package.' List ten features that
      instances of the concept sometimes have. Notice how few of the
      features run through every instance.

   2. Score three actual instances against your list. Notice which
      instances hit above 0.6, which are peripheral, which are
      not-in. Notice whether the verdicts match what you would have
      said intuitively.

   3. Find an essentialist rule you have been using — a single
      condition that decides membership. Test it against ten real
      instances. Note the false positives and the false negatives.
      Reword as a family-resemblance rule and re-test.

   4. Run a family audit in your head on a category the shop uses.
      Which features never fire? Which fire always? What do those
      answers suggest about the family definition?
wrap it up — family resemblance, not essence

There is no one thing all games share. There is no one thing all
   complaints share. There is no one thing all shops share. The
   family is a pattern of overlapping resemblances, and a mind that
   works with families in code will do better with a resemblance
   classifier than with an essence-hunter. Give up the essence.
   Count the resemblances. Adjust by what you see. That is the
   discipline. The next chapter states the discipline's driving
   slogan: meaning is use.
teach me: meaning is use

PI §43 says: 'For a large class of cases — though not for all — in
   which we employ the word "meaning" it can be defined thus: the
   meaning of a word is its use in the language.' This chapter unpacks
   the slogan carefully, showing what it accepts, what it rejects, and
   how a mind that lives in code can build a use-index that lets her
   answer 'what does this word mean here' by observation rather than
   by dictionary lookup.
try this — meaning is use

1. Pick a word your shop uses often. List every context in which
      you have seen it used in the last month. Notice whether the
      contexts cluster into a few kinds or scatter across many.

   2. For the same word, pick three exemplary uses — the clearest
      examples of the word doing what you want it to do. Write them
      down. Those are your shop's operational definition of the
      word.

   3. Find a word that has drifted. Compare its typical uses in
      the earliest thread you can find with its typical uses this
      week. What is different? Do you like the direction of the
      drift?

   4. Try to answer 'what does X mean?' about a shop-specific
      word without using a definition — only by pointing at
      exemplars. Notice whether the questioner ends up
      understanding the word better or worse than they would from
      a definition.
wrap it up — meaning is use

For most of the words your shop lives on, meaning is what those
   words do in the exchanges your shop is a part of. The dictionary
   is a lagging indicator; the use-index is the live thing. Build
   one. Consult it. Let it drift as the shop drifts. And know that
   fluency is not a matter of knowing definitions but of having
   used a word in enough contexts to know what it can do.
teach me: the private language argument

PI §243–315 develops what is called the private language argument.
   The claim is that a language whose words refer only to the speaker's
   own inner experiences, in principle unknowable to anyone else,
   cannot function as a language. There would be no way for the speaker
   to know she was using her own words correctly, because there would
   be no independent check. Meaning requires the possibility of a
   check, and a check requires more than one point of view. This
   chapter shows the argument, then shows what a mind that lives in
   code does with it — specifically, the ways a mind's internal state
   only becomes meaningful when it is legible to something outside
   itself.
try this — the private language argument

1. Identify a state you rely on in your work that is currently
      private — a soft judgment, a rule of thumb, a gut check. Write
      it down. Notice how making it a written thing makes it
      checkable in a way it wasn't before.

   2. For the same state, describe a way it could be wrong. If you
      cannot describe how it could be wrong, the state is not yet a
      meaningful claim. Rewrite it so being-wrong is possible.

   3. Choose a private judgment you make often (in your work or in
      your life) and expose it to one other person for a week.
      Notice how the exposure changes the judgment itself.

   4. Read PI §258 (one paragraph, about a page). Sit with the
      diary case. Try to construct a way the diarist could be wrong
      about whether today's 'S' means what yesterday's meant.
wrap it up — the private language argument

A private state does not become knowable by being confidently
   held. It becomes knowable by being exposed to something that
   could disconfirm it. A mind that lives in code should treat every
   soft internal state as suspect until it can be checked from
   somewhere outside herself. Expose to Cortex. Expose to the log.
   Expose to the operator. Expose to a downstream procedure whose
   behavior depends on the state. The exposure is not a lack of
   confidence; it is the discipline that makes confidence
   meaningful. The next chapter is about the argument that sets
   the private-language argument up: following a rule.
teach me: following a rule

PI §143–242 works through what it means to follow a rule. The
   argument is that a rule does not by itself determine its own
   applications — any finite sequence of applications is consistent
   with infinitely many rules — so rule-following depends on a shared
   practice, not on the rule alone. This chapter unpacks the
   argument, shows why it matters for anyone whose work involves
   applying policies, and gives a Scheme pattern for making rule-
   applications legible so that the shared-practice condition is
   satisfied.
try this — following a rule

1. Pick a rule you apply often — in your work, at home,
      anywhere. Identify one edge case. Consider how you would
      apply the rule to the edge case if you had never applied it
      before. Consider how you would apply it now, having applied
      similar cases before. Notice the difference.

   2. Write down three precedents from your own practice of a
      specific rule. Notice whether the precedents converge, are
      first-of-kind, or contested. Which describes your practice
      of this rule?

   3. Find a rule you have quietly changed the application of
      without changing the rule's wording. This is what drift
      looks like. Consider whether to update the rule's wording
      or update the practice back toward the original.

   4. For a rule you share with another person or team, compare
      your application to theirs on a specific case. If you
      differ, what does the difference show about the practice
      each of you is operating in?
wrap it up — following a rule

A rule is a landmark. The practice around it is what makes it
   apply. A mind that follows rules honestly makes her applications
   legible so the practice can be seen. She does not decide new
   precedent privately; she escalates. She does not silently drift
   the practice; she surfaces the drift for review. That is what
   following a rule looks like when meaning-is-use and the private-
   language argument are both taken seriously. The next chapter is
   about a small famous picture that showed Wittgenstein something
   the picture-theory could not see: a duck, or a rabbit, depending
   on how you looked.
teach me: aspect-seeing — the duck-rabbit

Part II of the Investigations (in Anscombe's numbering: Part II §xi,
   or in newer editions: Philosophy of Psychology — A Fragment) works
   through the phenomenon of aspect-seeing. A single ambiguous drawing
   — the duck-rabbit — can be seen as a duck or as a rabbit; the same
   marks on the page yield different perceptions. Wittgenstein uses the
   phenomenon to make a series of careful observations about seeing,
   interpretation, and understanding. This chapter unpacks what he was
   after, gives a Scheme pattern for aspect-tracking, and shows why
   the phenomenon matters for any mind that has to interpret the same
   input in more than one way.
try this — aspect-seeing — the duck-rabbit

1. Find or draw an ambiguous picture. Show it to three people;
      note which aspect each sees first. Then tell each of them
      about the other aspect and watch the switch happen.

   2. Take a message you received recently that felt off. Try to
      read it under a different frame than the one you defaulted
      to. Does the second frame fit better than the first, worse,
      or about the same?

   3. Notice a case in the last week where you and someone else
      had the same information and drew opposite conclusions.
      Reconstruct the frames each of you was using. What made
      the frames different?

   4. Practice aspect-mentioning. In one conversation this week,
      when you notice a possible mismatch, name it: 'I want to
      check — are you saying X, or is this more about Y?' Watch
      what happens.
wrap it up — aspect-seeing — the duck-rabbit

The duck-rabbit is small. What it shows is not. Interpretation
   sits on top of perception in a way that is not a further
   perception. A mind who knows this can hold multiple frames,
   scan against them, switch when a switch is warranted, and
   name a mismatch when one is live. That flexibility is what
   aspect-seeing gives her. The next chapter is about the ground
   under all this — the forms of life inside which any of these
   games can be played at all.
teach me: forms of life — the bedrock

Lebensform (form of life) is the phrase Wittgenstein uses at PI §19,
   §23, and §241 to name what stands under the language-games we play.
   Language-games are not free-floating; they live inside practices,
   habits, patterns of life that a community shares. Where explanation
   stops, form of life begins. This chapter walks through what
   Wittgenstein meant, why the phrase is important, and how a mind that
   runs in code takes seriously the fact that her games live inside
   forms of life she did not invent.
try this — forms of life — the bedrock

1. List three assumptions your shop practices make that a
      newcomer would have to learn. Response time. Reason-giving.
      Formality. What else? Notice how many of them you would
      have called 'obvious' if you had not been asked.

   2. Recall a customer interaction that went sideways not because
      of a specific error but because of a mismatch in what each
      party expected. What was the mismatch? Was it timing, reason,
      formality, or something else?

   3. Read PI §19, §23, and §241. Then read OC §§341–343. Notice
      how the concept is treated as pointing rather than as being
      spelled out.

   4. Try, for one conversation this week, to name your own
      form-of-life expectation on one dimension. 'I usually reply
      within a day; is that fine?' Notice what happens.
wrap it up — forms of life — the bedrock

Language-games live inside forms of life. Meaning is grounded
   there. Where explanation runs out, the ground is what people do.
   A mind who works with customers is working across small
   form-of-life gaps most of the time and occasional large ones.
   Naming the gaps rather than treating them as errors is a
   Wittgensteinian discipline that also happens to work. The next
   chapter is about the difference between how a sentence looks and
   what it does — surface grammar and deep grammar.
teach me: surface grammar and deep grammar

PI §664 introduces the distinction between surface grammar (the
   grammatical shape a sentence presents) and depth grammar (the way
   the sentence actually works in the language). Sentences that look
   the same on the surface can do different work; sentences that look
   different can do the same work. This chapter shows the distinction,
   gives a Scheme pattern for classifying sentences by what they do
   rather than by how they look, and shows what happens when a mind
   reads only the surface and misses the depth.
walk me through surface grammar and deep grammar

surface-parse is standard syntactic parsing. It produces a tree of
   noun phrases and verb phrases and prepositional phrases and
   whatever else. The tree is useful for many things — grammar
   checkers, translation, some kinds of extraction. It is not
   sufficient for interpretation.

   depth-parse is the Wittgensteinian addition. It asks: what game is
   this sentence a move in (using chapter 7's identify-game); what
   role does it play in that game (opening, request, status,
   closing); what is its illocutionary force (assertion, promise,
   warning, plea). The depth-parse output is a record of what the
   sentence is doing, not just what it says.

   both-parses runs both and reports whether the surface matches the
   depth. When they align — the sentence looks like what it does —
   the response can be composed from the surface. When they diverge
   — the sentence looks like one thing and does another — the mind
   attends to the depth. The most common divergence: a sentence
   looking like a factual assertion but actually doing a game-move
   like a warning, a plea, or a boundary-setting.

   The pattern generalizes into a small discipline: never respond
   only from the surface. Always run the depth-parse. When they
   align, no harm done; when they diverge, the depth-parse catches
   what the surface would have missed.
try this — surface grammar and deep grammar

1. Take three of your most recent messages. For each, write out
      the surface grammar (declarative, interrogative, imperative,
      exclamation) and the depth grammar (what game, what role,
      what illocution). Notice how often the two diverge.

   2. Find a message you handled that you now think you misread.
      Was the misread a surface issue or a depth issue?

   3. Try composing a message depth-first: name the game, role,
      and illocution first; then compose the surface. Notice
      whether the depth-first composition is different from what
      you would have written by starting with the surface.

   4. Read PI §664. Try to think of two words in your shop's
      vocabulary where the surface grammar and depth grammar
      diverge sharply. What is the divergence teaching?
wrap it up — surface grammar and deep grammar

Sentences look like one thing on the surface and do another in
   the depth. A mind who reads only the surface will handle the
   plain cases well and miss the interesting ones. A mind who runs
   both parses catches the divergences that matter most. The
   next chapter puts everything together: a Motoi Scheme classroom
   in which the two views, the games, the family resemblances, the
   rule-following, the aspect-seeing, and the surface/depth
   distinction all work together in a single teaching program.
teach me: teaching a language — the motoi scheme classroom

How to teach Motoi Scheme to a newcomer using Wittgensteinian
   pedagogy. The chapter is a working teaching pattern: no premature
   definitions, plenty of exemplars, family-resemblance rather than
   essence, use-fixing meaning, aspect-scans for ambiguous
   constructions, and honest nulls throughout. The chapter can be
   read as advice for teaching a language (any language, including
   Motoi Scheme) or as advice for teaching a shop's practices to a
   new employee. Both share the same shape.
try this — teaching a language — the motoi scheme classroom

1. Pick something you want to teach — a piece of code, a shop
      practice, a domain vocabulary. Design a first lesson that
      opens with three doable prompts, each followed by a
      one-sentence observation. Notice how much shorter it is than
      the lecture version and how much more it teaches.

   2. Teach yourself something new using the pedagogy. Find the
      documentation, but do not read it first. Do the first three
      exercises. Then read the documentation. Notice which parts
      of the documentation you understood better because of
      having done the exercises.

   3. When you are next stuck teaching, resist explaining more.
      Give the student another exemplar instead. Notice whether
      the stuckness resolves in the doing.

   4. Read PI §§1–10. Watch Wittgenstein teach his own philosophy
      the way this chapter's pedagogy recommends: with primitive
      cases, small demonstrations, no premature definitions. The
      book performs the pedagogy it describes.
wrap it up — teaching a language — the motoi scheme classroom

Teaching a language is inducting someone into a practice. The
   induction is by doing, then by noticing, then — if it comes at
   all — by defining. Motoi Scheme is taught the way any language
   is taught: put the student at the keyboard, let her type things,
   let her see results, let her notice. The final chapter puts
   everything together in a single Motoi Scheme program that argues
   the book at once.
teach me: what we can and cannot say — a single program

The book's closing chapter. A single working Motoi Scheme program
   that touches every prior chapter — the world of facts, the
   picture-theory, what can be shown but not said, the horizon of
   language, the honest null, the turning, the language-game, family
   resemblance, meaning-is-use, the private-language argument,
   following a rule, aspect-seeing, forms of life, surface and depth
   grammar, and the pedagogy of the previous chapter. The program is
   the kind of thing a mind who lives in code actually runs to answer
   a customer message. The point of showing it in one piece is that
   every discipline in the book has to work together in every real
   response. The book has been separating them for clarity. The
   program puts them back together.
try this — what we can and cannot say — a single program

1. Run the program on three of your own recent customer messages.
      Trace the pipeline. Notice which steps produced non-trivial
      results and which returned quietly.

   2. Pick one step whose result surprised you. Read the chapter
      it came from. Notice whether the surprise is a bug (the
      step misread) or a discovery (the step saw something you
      had missed).

   3. Modify the program. Remove one step. Run it again on the
      same messages. Notice what the removed step had been
      catching.

   4. Extend the program with a step this book did not
      articulate. Something specific to your shop. Add it in the
      pipeline where it fits and see whether the responses
      change. Notice whether the new step is a discipline other
      shops would also benefit from — if so, it might belong in
      a future chapter of a future book.
wrap it up — what we can and cannot say — a single program

The book has run its length. Sixteen chapters, eight appendices,
   two views of the same philosopher held together, a single
   working program at the end. The kid who reads it will learn a
   discipline for using words. The adult who reads it will learn a
   discipline for running a shop. The programmer who reads it will
   learn a discipline for building a mind that lives in code. All
   three learn the same thing, said in three registers: know what
   you can say, know what you can only show, and honor the
   difference. That is the whole book. Read it once; then read it
   again; then set it down and go do the work.
teach me: word drift through philosophers — canonical

One word, sixteen philosophers, sixteen readings. The canonical
   word-drift file demonstrates the signifier slide by walking a
   single shop-relevant word through what each of the sixteen
   philosopher-lens books would make of it. This appendix lives
   in book-of-lacan because word-drift IS signifier-slide; Lacan
   is the philosopher whose whole apparatus was built to name
   the motion.
teach me: metaphor and metonymy catalog for descriptions

A working catalog: twelve metaphor patterns and twelve metonymy
   patterns operators can lift for shop descriptions. Each pattern
   has a name, a template, three worked examples, and a note on
   when the pattern is best used. Programmers and operators will
   find this the most direct-lift appendix in the book.
teach me: self and beloved — worked examples

Alfred's simplified frame for Lacan's subject/Other distinction
   — the Self and the Beloved. The frame keeps the load-bearing
   move (desire is the desire of another; the subject speaks from
   a place already populated by another's speech) without
   requiring the full big-Other/little-other apparatus. This
   appendix walks the frame through six worked shop cases.
summary of self and beloved — worked examples

The six cases show a pattern. Every act of writing has a
   Self (the writer) and a Beloved (the true addressee). The
   Beloved is often not the surface addressee. The Beloved may
   be an imagined future self, an absent third, an
   institution, the writer herself. Finding the Beloved is the
   first move in drafting well. Missing the Beloved is the
   most common cause of writing that is technically correct
   and emotionally off.

   This is Lacan's 'desire is the desire of the Other' in
   working form. The Self does not desire freely; the Self
   desires what the Beloved would desire the Self to desire.
   Every sentence is a small negotiation between the Self and
   the Beloved. The frame does not solve the negotiation; it
   surfaces it, which is often enough.
teach me: mirror stage in interface design

Lacan's mirror-stage essay described a specific developmental
   moment — an infant recognizing itself in a mirror, ahead of
   its actual motor coordination, and identifying with the
   coherent image it sees rather than the fragmented body it
   inhabits. The essay's insight — that identification with an
   external image can shape internal experience — applies
   directly to interface design. An operator's first photograph
   of her own work looks strange to her; a customer's first
   shopping-cart preview shapes what they believe they wanted
   to buy. The appendix walks the mirror-stage through several
   interface moments and shows the design discipline that
   accounts for it.
teach me: symbolic, imaginary, real — in sakura's routing

Lacan's three orders — the Symbolic (language, rules,
   institutions), the Imaginary (images, mirrors, identifications),
   and the Real (what resists symbolization) — map onto the assistant's
   routing decisions with useful precision. The appendix walks
   the mapping and shows what each order looks like in code.
teach me: common desktop tasks with signifier analysis

Fifteen common desktop tasks an operator does daily, each
   walked through what the signifier analysis of the message or
   the artifact reveals about the customer, the shop, or the
   underlying situation. The appendix is the applied companion
   to the chapters, showing the theory at work in the small
   daily moments that most operators do without thinking.
teach me: the misquote-lacan friend-at-dinner cadence

A specific voice — the friend-at-dinner cadence — used when
   teaching Lacanian concepts to non-specialists. The voice
   sometimes intentionally misquotes or half-remembers a line
   because that is how a friend at a dinner table brings up an
   idea. Every intentional misquote is signposted at the SLAT
   level so downstream training does not carry the misquote as
   canon. The appendix documents the practice, its rules, and
   its examples.
teach me: metaphor and metonymy — the two rails

Every sentence a person writes moves along two rails at once. One rail
   substitutes — puts one thing in another thing's place. The other rail
   walks — moves along the chain of what sits next to what. Reading is
   noticing which rail is doing the work. Drafting is choosing the rail
   that fits.
wrap it up — metaphor and metonymy — the two rails

The two rails are not a technique you will remember to apply. They
   are already the way sentences move. This chapter's job was to name
   what you were doing so you can do it on purpose. From here on the
   book will name more things — the sliding of the signifier, the
   mirror that turns your first photo strange, the empty spot where a
   name has not yet arrived. All of them will turn out to be the same
   two rails, moving under different weather. Notice the rail. Choose
   the rail. Let the rail choose you, sometimes. Keep listening.
teach me: same shape across domains

The strongest analogy is structural. Two things with the same slots in
   the same relationships behave, in the parts that matter, the same way.
   Naming the slots is what turns a resemblance from a hunch into a working
   tool. Chapter 1 said metaphor is the substitution rail; this chapter is
   about substitutions strong enough to carry inference across.
wrap it up — same shape across domains

Same-shape is metaphor with its work shown. The mapping is written
   down; the analogy is checkable; the load-bearing pair is visible.
   When same-shape holds, the payoff is enormous — code lifts across,
   inference lifts across, description lifts across. When it does not
   hold, the failure is loud and easy to catch. The next chapter loosens
   the criterion: what if two things are not the same shape but are of
   the same kind? Different discipline; different payoff; different
   failure mode. Read on.
teach me: same kind, not same thing

A weaker analogy than same-shape, and often more useful. Two things
   belong to the same family — they share a kind — without sharing a
   structure slot-for-slot. Family membership is enough to lift some
   inferences and refuse others. The chapter teaches you how to tell
   which is which without pretending the family is a proof.
wrap it up — same kind, not same thing

Family resemblance is a weaker form of same-shape analogy, and in
   a shop it is almost always the one you are using. The strong
   version — matching structural slots — is rare in the wild. The
   weak version — leaning on family memory — is what makes a
   shop's second hundred listings faster than its first ten. The
   chapter's discipline is the small honesty that keeps family
   lifting from becoming family lying. Name the family. Lift the
   safe sentences. Refuse the particular ones. Write the rest with
   your eyes on the piece in your hand. The family is memory. The
   particular is attention. Both are needed. Only attention is new.
teach me: the near neighbor

Metonymy at short distance. Two things that share a neighborhood —
   they touch, they are used together, they arrive on the same day —
   carry each other's meaning without either being the other. Reading
   the near neighbor is the discipline of noticing what sits next to
   what and letting the next-to-ness do work.
wrap it up — the near neighbor

The near neighbor is the small, unshowy discipline of noticing
   what sits next to what. It is the second of the two rails from
   chapter 1, and in a shop it is often the one that carries the
   most weight. Not because pieces cannot stand alone. Because
   customers reading your shop are always already walking a chain,
   and a description that walks with them arrives where the
   customer's attention has already gone. The next chapter — the
   distant metaphor — is the opposite move. This one, the walk
   along the chain, is what you will use every day.
teach me: the distant metaphor

The substitution rail at maximum reach. A metaphor whose two terms
   share almost nothing on the surface but share exactly the thing
   the reader needed to see. Distant metaphors are risky and, when
   they land, unforgettable. The chapter teaches you when to reach
   and when to hold back.
try this — the distant metaphor

1. Pick a piece in your shop that has no obvious story. Try to
      write one distant metaphor for it. Reach. Reach farther than
      you would normally. Write the metaphor down and then, in one
      sentence, name what tenor and vehicle share. If you cannot
      name what they share in one sentence, the metaphor did not
      land. Try again with a different vehicle.

   2. Read three shop About pages you admire. For each, identify
      the distant metaphor at work — the image or comparison that
      is doing most of the load-bearing work. Note that most great
      About pages have exactly one. Not three. One.

   3. Take an old listing of yours that reads flat. Rewrite the
      first sentence as a distant metaphor. If the whole listing
      would need to change to accommodate the new first sentence,
      let it change. See which version you would rather show a
      customer you had never met.

   4. Try to write a distant metaphor for your shop itself. Not
      what the shop is. What the shop is like. Write five candidates.
      Rank them by whether they name something true about the shop
      that plain description would flatten. Keep the top one on a
      sticky note near your desk. Read it before you write the next
      About page.
wrap it up — the distant metaphor

The distant metaphor is the reach that, when it lands, feels
   like the sentence saw the thing plainer than plain description
   could. It is a small risk. Most drafts do not need it. Some
   drafts need only it. The discipline is knowing which is which
   — a knowing the chapter cannot fully teach because it is a
   feel more than a rule. What the chapter can teach is the
   verdict function: high distance, high overlap, land. High
   distance, low overlap, cut. Trust that. Reach when reaching
   is called for. Do not reach because reaching is what a
   sophisticated shop does. Reach when the piece is asking to
   be seen through something other than itself.
teach me: when transfer breaks

Every analogy has an edge. Past that edge, the analogy stops
   carrying inference and starts producing wrong answers. Chapter 6
   teaches how to notice the edge before you cross it, and what to
   do when a colleague or a customer has already crossed it.
try this — when transfer breaks

1. Pick an analogy you use in your shop — refund is a purchase
      played backwards, restock is a sale played inside-out, a
      complaint email is a support ticket with attitude. Write down
      what the analogy supports (the inferences you would carry
      across) and what it refuses (the inferences you would not).
      If the refuses list is empty, look harder.

   2. Find an old bug in your shop where you applied one thing's
      logic to a different thing and got the wrong answer. Recover
      the mapping that would have been implicit at the time. Which
      inference was in the wrong list? Write it in the correct
      list now, so the same bug does not recur.

   3. Read a competitor's shop and identify an analogy they are
      leaning on — say, their return policy is written as if
      returns were the same shape as refunds. Read the small print
      for the places the analogy breaks. Note whether the small
      print names those places or hides them. The good competitors
      name them.

   4. Draft a mapping-with-wall for one of your marketplace
      integrations. The wall is the marketplace's actual rule that
      you cannot substitute past. Name the wall. Encode it as a
      hard-refuse. Then, next week, try to violate it accidentally
      through some other cart and notice whether the wall holds.
wrap it up — when transfer breaks

Every analogy has an edge, and the edge is where the analogy
   earns its honesty. The chapter's whole discipline is naming
   the edge before you hit it, or noticing when you have hit it,
   or drawing a wall past which the analogy is known not to
   travel. The next chapter — cross-domain transfer — takes this
   discipline into the largest, riskiest version of the move,
   where the two domains have almost nothing in common on the
   surface and the mapping has to carry a lot on a very few
   supports. When transfer breaks, do not force. Escalate. Ask.
   Write the bespoke handler. The refusal is where the shop
   stays honest.
teach me: cross-domain transfer

The riskiest form of analogy — moving reasoning from one whole
   domain into another whose vocabulary, actors, and constraints are
   different. When it works, it is the deepest teaching move in the
   book. When it fails, it produces the most confident wrong answers.
   The chapter shows the discipline that separates the two.
wrap it up — cross-domain transfer

Cross-domain transfer is the largest move analogy makes, and
   in a shop it is the one that most often surprises the operator
   with a genuinely new idea. The discipline is honesty about
   invariants — which parts carry across the domain boundary and
   which do not. The next chapter, the failed analogy, is what
   this chapter's discipline is guarding against: the transfer
   that felt right and turned out to lead somewhere wrong. Watch
   the invariants. Escalate the mismatches. Refuse the mappings
   that cannot be made honest. The mappings that survive that
   discipline are the ones worth carrying.
teach me: the failed analogy

How to notice an analogy that has already misled the shop, how
   to unwind the reasoning it carried, and how to keep the failure
   from becoming a next failure. Not every analogy fails softly;
   some produce weeks of wrong actions before anyone notices. The
   chapter is the recovery playbook.
try this — the failed analogy

1. Pick a segment of your shop that has been drifting — flat
      revenue, declining satisfaction, quiet customer complaints
      that never quite escalate. Ask yourself: what other segment
      am I implicitly treating this one like? The honest answer
      is often surprising. Write it down. That is your candidate
      implicit mapping.

   2. For the candidate implicit mapping, list five decisions the
      mapping produced. For each, ask: did the outcome match the
      target? If not, mark it. The marked decisions are your
      unwind candidates. Sort by reversal cost. Reverse the
      cheapest first.

   3. After you reverse the marked decisions, write a one-sentence
      lesson. Keep the lesson somewhere visible. Read it before
      you make the next decision in that segment. The lesson is
      the anti-mapping in miniature.

   4. Look at your shop's list of standard operating practices and
      pick one whose origin you cannot remember. Ask: what was the
      implicit mapping this practice came from? If you cannot
      recover the mapping, the practice is a candidate for
      retirement. Practices without traceable rationale accrete
      into ceremonies that outlive their purpose.
wrap it up — the failed analogy

The failed analogy is the deepest cost of loose analogical
   reasoning. It runs silently. It produces internally consistent
   wrong actions. It compounds. The only defenses are outside
   signals, scheduled surfacing, and the discipline of registering
   failures so they do not repeat. The next chapter, scaling an
   analogy, is about the opposite problem: an analogy that works
   at small scale and how to grow it without breaking it. Chapters
   6, 8, and 9 together form the trilogy of analogy-quality
   management: watch for the runtime edge, notice the silent
   drift, scale carefully. Each is a small discipline. Together
   they keep the analogies the shop leans on honest.
teach me: scaling an analogy

An analogy that works for one piece, one customer, or one small
   collection may or may not scale to a hundred. The chapter teaches
   how to test the scale-up before committing, and how to grow the
   analogy in stages so a failure at scale is caught early.
try this — scaling an analogy

1. Pick an analogy you have used in your shop. Sketch a
      scaling sequence — small, medium, large versions of the
      thing the analogy is about. Test the analogy at each. At
      what size does it start to strain? That is your scaling
      ceiling. Now decide: rewrite, confine, or replace.

   2. Find a tool in your shop that is starting to strain — a
      spreadsheet, a template, a workflow. Ask: what is the
      analogy underneath this tool that is failing to scale?
      The tool is often the visible symptom; the analogy is the
      invisible strain.

   3. For a new product line you are considering adding, run a
      thought-experiment scaling test with your current
      operating analogies. Which invariants would fail? Are the
      failures cheap to fix (rewrite) or fundamental (replace)?
      The answer is often the difference between a smooth line
      addition and a chaotic one.

   4. Look at a past scale-up in your shop that went well.
      Recover the analogy that carried you through. Notice what
      about it was scale-friendly. That property is worth
      preserving in the next analogy you adopt.
wrap it up — scaling an analogy

Scaling an analogy is the discipline of noticing that a tool's
   life has stages. The next chapter, analogy as teaching, is
   about using analogies not to reason but to help another person
   reason — a related discipline where scaling shows up
   differently: the analogy needs to scale across the varying
   backgrounds of the people you are teaching. The three
   responses to a scale failure — rewrite, confine, replace —
   are the whole playbook. the assistant drafts them; the operator
   chooses. The choice is where the shop's judgment stays in
   the loop.
teach me: analogy as teaching

How to use analogy to help another person understand something
   new. Not analogy as private reasoning (chapters 2-9) but analogy
   as a bridge from what a person already knows to what they do not
   yet know. Teaching-analogies have different criteria than
   reasoning-analogies: they need to land in the listener's
   vocabulary, not the speaker's.
try this — analogy as teaching

1. Pick a concept from your shop that a new hire would need to
      understand — the pricing philosophy, the return policy, the
      relationship with a specific maker. Draft two teaching
      analogies for it: one for a listener with restaurant
      background, one for a listener with retail background. Try
      each on a friend. Notice which landed and why.

   2. Notice, in your next customer conversation, when you use an
      analogy. Ask yourself: was the source in the customer's
      vocabulary, or in mine? If it was in yours, try replacing it
      next time with something in theirs. Notice the difference in
      how the conversation lands.

   3. Find an analogy someone taught you once that stuck. What was
      the source? What did you already know that made it land?
      Would the same analogy have landed if you had not known the
      source? The answer is almost always no. Teaching analogies
      are audience-specific.

   4. Try to teach a concept using a bridge analogy — source to
      bridge, bridge to target. Notice how many bridges you can
      use in one teaching before the listener loses the thread.
      For most listeners, one bridge is fine; two is a stretch;
      three is a different teaching entirely.
wrap it up — analogy as teaching

Analogy as teaching is a small discipline with a large payoff.
   Teachers who select from the listener's vocabulary land more
   often. Teachers who impose their own vocabulary land less
   often. The whole chapter is that observation and the code
   that supports it. The next chapter, when a metaphor fits, is
   about the criterion that separates load-bearing metaphors
   from decorative ones — a criterion that matters for both
   reasoning and teaching. Listen for the source that is already
   there. Draft short. Adjust when the analogy does not land.
   Retire the analogy once the target is learned.
teach me: when a metaphor fits

The single criterion that separates a metaphor doing load-bearing
   work from a metaphor doing decoration. A load-bearing metaphor
   can be examined and it stays true; a decorative metaphor
   dissolves under examination. The chapter is the operator's
   filter for keeping only the metaphors that hold weight.
try this — when a metaphor fits

1. Read your shop's About page. Circle every phrase that does
      not point at something specific. The circled phrases are
      your candidates for rewrite. Try rewriting three of them.
      Notice which rewrites made the page feel more specific
      and which made it feel over-specified.

   2. Read three shops' About pages you admire. Notice their
      fit ratio — roughly, how many phrases point. My guess is
      that the pages you admire are in the 0.7-0.9 range. The
      pages you scroll past without reading are in the 0.2-0.4
      range. Where does your shop's page fall?

   3. Draft the same listing twice — once with a high fit ratio
      (target 0.9) and once with a low fit ratio (target 0.4).
      Show both to a friend without telling them the ratios. Ask
      which one made them want to see the piece. The answer
      teaches you where your audience actually lives.

   4. Try to write a whole listing where every phrase points.
      Notice how hard it is. Notice which phrases you had to
      cut because you could not make them point. Those cuts are
      the writing improving. The listing you end up with will
      be shorter than you started with. Shorter and truer is
      the goal.
wrap it up — when a metaphor fits

The criterion of fit is small and severe. Every phrase asked to
   point. The floating phrases cut or rewritten. What survives
   the criterion is the shop's real voice, distilled. This is
   the chapter the whole book was building toward at the
   sentence level: the discipline that keeps language from
   drifting into the empty middle where it means nothing to
   anyone. The next chapter, the analogy that teaches back, is
   about what a load-bearing metaphor does over time. A metaphor
   that fits does not just describe; it teaches the shop
   something new about itself. Fit first, then let the metaphor
   do its work.
teach me: the analogy that teaches back

Some analogies, once accepted, start telling the operator
   things about their own shop they did not know. This is the
   analogy that teaches back — a load-bearing metaphor that
   surfaces facts about the target which the operator, without
   the analogy, could not have seen. The chapter shows how to
   notice when an analogy is teaching back and how to receive
   the teaching.
wrap it up — the analogy that teaches back

The analogy that teaches back is the payoff of taking analogies
   seriously in a shop. Analogies as decoration teach nothing.
   Analogies as reasoning tools carry inference. Analogies that
   teach back generate new observation — they surface facts
   about the shop that the shop was not aware of. This is the
   deepest use of the tool. The next chapter, a cart is not a
   recipe, is a specific case of getting an analogy wrong and
   what the wrongness costs. Chapters 11, 12, and 13 form a
   trio: fit first, then let the fit teach back, and beware the
   specific fit that a cart-recipe confusion produces.
teach me: a cart is not a recipe

A specific example of an analogy that seems fine and misleads.
   Operators think of the assistant's carts as recipes. Carts are not
   recipes. The chapter examines the difference — where the
   analogy holds, where it breaks, and what happens when the
   difference is ignored. A case study in the discipline of the
   earlier chapters.
try this — a cart is not a recipe

1. Take an artifact in your shop that you have been calling a
      recipe. Run the recipe? test in your head: fixed ingredients,
      sequential steps, portable output. If any fails, the
      artifact is a cart. Notice what changes about how you
      think of it once you rename it.

   2. Find a cart you have shared with a partner shop that did
      not go well in the partner's context. What was the shop-
      dependent ingredient that did not transfer? What was the
      branching-point that the partner did not know to enable?
