   draft this and hand it to the operator for review, always. The
   operator ships or edits.
try this — the look — the gaze of the other

1. Remember a moment recently when you felt looked at — not
      literally, but Sartrean. A moment where you became aware
      of yourself as an object in someone's field.

   2. Notice what your body did in that moment. Notice what your
      first impulse was — to explain, to hide, to correct, to
      lash out.

   3. Notice that the first impulse was not chosen. It was a
      reflex. That reflex is what hold-the-moment is trying to
      insert a pause into.

   4. Next time a Look-shaped event lands — a critical email, a
      curt reply, a review — practice holding for a breath.
      Then longer. Then a walk. Then reply.

   5. Notice whether the responses composed from steadiness
      land better than the responses composed from flinch.

   6. Do not train yourself to be unmoved by Looks. That is the
      opposite pathology. The point is to feel the Look and to
      respond from somewhere other than the flinch.

   7. Read chapter 9 (anguish) next. It is the mode Sartre thinks
      accompanies awareness of freedom, and it composes with the
      Look in interesting ways.
wrap it up — the look — the gaze of the other

The Look is the specific event in which another consciousness
   makes you into an object in their field. It happens constantly
   in a shop and mostly at low intensity. Occasionally it lands
   hard. The discipline is not to be untouched by it — that is a
   different pathology — but to hold the moment long enough that
   the response comes from steadiness. the assistant detects, holds,
   drafts, surfaces. The operator writes the reply from the held
   moment. That is the whole shape.
teach me: anguish — the vertigo of freedom

Anguish (angoisse) in Sartre is the emotional coloring of consciousness
   when it becomes aware of its own freedom. It is not fear of an external
   thing. It is the vertigo you get when you notice that no one else is
   going to decide the next act for you. This chapter teaches the reader
   (and the assistant) to recognize anguish as the ordinary shape it usually
   takes, not to flee it, and not to let it become an excuse for
   inaction.
break down anguish — the vertigo of freedom

anguish-signal-check looks for the ordinary form anguish takes
   in a workday. It is not looking for people crying at their
   desks. It is looking for stuck drafts and decision loops. Both
   are common. Both are often anguish showing up in ordinary
   clothes.

   The check is conservative. Stuck drafts and decision loops
   have many causes — the operator might be tired, might be
   waiting on information, might be legitimately stuck on a hard
   problem. Anguish is one possibility among several. The check
   does not need to identify anguish specifically to be useful;
   it just needs to notice the shape and offer support.

   gentle-support produces a small non-diagnostic offer. 'Want me
   to draft three versions?' is not a lecture; it is a hand.
   'Want to set this down till Wednesday?' is not therapy; it is
   a scheduling suggestion. The offers help the person keep
   moving without requiring them to accept a philosophical
   framing.

   diagnose-as-anguish is the same null-implementation pattern as
   authenticity-score in chapter 4. It exists to document a
   commitment: the assistant will not tell the operator he is in
   existential anguish. That would be an intrusion. the assistant's role
   is companionship, not diagnosis.
try this — anguish — the vertigo of freedom

1. Recall a moment recently when you noticed you were in
      charge of the next thing you did and no one was going to
      decide for you. Not a big life decision. Just a small
      one — what to eat, what to reply to, when to leave.

   2. Notice the small vertigo, if there was one. It is often
      subtle. Sometimes it is a beat of hesitation, a slight
      constriction, a sense of the moment being larger than it
      seemed.

   3. Do not try to make it go away. Do not try to make it
      bigger. Just notice it.

   4. Notice that on the other side of the vertigo, you acted
      anyway. You made the sandwich or sent the reply or left.
      The vertigo did not stop you.

   5. This is what living with anguish looks like. It comes,
      you notice, you act. It is not a problem to be solved.
      It is the texture of freedom.

   6. Read chapter 10 (nausea) next. It is a related mode with
      a different flavor — awareness of contingency rather than
      awareness of freedom — and it composes with anguish in
      interesting ways.
wrap it up — anguish — the vertigo of freedom

Anguish is the emotional coloring of consciousness noticing its
   own freedom. It is not a pathology. It is not a signal that you
   have done something wrong. It is what accurate self-perception
   feels like. Most days it does not land; some days it does. When
   it lands, you notice, and you act anyway. the assistant's job is to
   help the person keep moving with small offers that do not
   diagnose. She never names the philosophical shape. She offers
   drafts. She offers reschedules. She offers a hand.
teach me: nausea — the raw contingency of being

Nausea (la nausee) in Sartre is the specific feeling of confronting
   the raw contingency of being — the fact that things exist without
   reason, that categories are human impositions, that the world was
   not made to be intelligible. This chapter teaches the reader (and
   the assistant) to recognize the shape when it lands, and — differently
   from anguish, which composes with action — to sit with nausea
   rather than fleeing it into busywork.
try this — nausea — the raw contingency of being

1. Recall a moment when something ordinary suddenly seemed
      arbitrary. The word 'chair.' The category 'lunch.' The
      idea of 'a country.' Not a philosophical seminar. An
      ordinary daily moment.

   2. Notice the feeling that accompanied it. Sartre's word is
      nausea. Your word can be whatever fits.

   3. Notice that the feeling passed. You went back to sitting
      in the chair, eating the lunch, being in the country.
      The layer smoothed back over.

   4. Notice that the layer is not less true for being a layer.
      The chair is really the sitting-thing. The lunch is really
      the meal. The country is really the political entity. The
      layers work because people keep using them. The
      contingency underneath does not invalidate the layers.

   5. If you can hold both — the layer works AND the contingency
      is real — you are close to what Sartre was after.

   6. Read chapter 11 (situated freedom) next. It is the other
      side of nausea: given that the world is contingent, how
      do you nonetheless act inside a specific situation?
teach me: situated freedom — facticity and the given

Sartre's freedom is not freedom from constraint. It is freedom inside
   constraint. Facticity — the given of one's situation, body, history,
   income, weather, health — is what freedom operates on. There is no
   freedom outside a situation. This chapter teaches the reader (and
   the assistant) to hold the two together: acknowledge the constraints
   accurately, and locate the choices that remain.
try this — situated freedom — facticity and the given

1. Pick a decision you are currently facing. Write it in one
      sentence at the top of a page.

   2. Under it, in the left column, write the constraints you
      believe you are working inside. Be honest. Include the
      real ones — rent, time, health, contract. Do not include
      preferences you are calling constraints.

   3. In the right column, write the choices you can see inside
      those constraints. Include the small ones. Include the
      ones you don't like.

   4. Look at the two columns. Are any of the left-column items
      possibly folded choices? Move them to the right column,
      tentatively, and see if the decision looks different.

   5. Now decide. The point of the exercise is not to have more
      options. It is to have accurate ones. Decide from the
      accurate map, not from the inflated or deflated one.

   6. Read chapter 12 (the project) next. It is the frame inside
      which all these small decisions add up to a life.
wrap it up — situated freedom — facticity and the given

Situated freedom is freedom inside constraint. Facticity is the
   given: rent, body, history, weather. Choices are what remain
   inside the given. Both are real. Both need naming. The operator
   who names constraints accurately and locates choices inside
   them acts well. The operator who inflates constraints or denies
   them acts less well. the assistant's job is to help produce accurate
   maps. She does not decide. She lays out the two columns and
   the operator chooses. That is the whole shape.
teach me: the project — the fundamental choice of self

The project (le projet) is Sartre's name for the fundamental orientation
   a person sustains across time. It is not a plan. It is the shape a
   life takes when small choices consistently point in the same direction.
   The project is not chosen once and then executed. It is chosen and
   re-chosen in every small act. This chapter is the long arc of the
   book. It teaches the reader (and the assistant) to see the project as it
   actually is — always contingent, always sustained, never finished.
try this — the project — the fundamental choice of self

1. Write, in one sentence, what you would say your project is
      right now. Not what you meant it to be. What you would
      say if a friend asked at dinner.

   2. Now look at the last three months of your calendar. What
      have you actually been doing?

   3. Do the acts of the last three months add up to the project
      you wrote in step one? Or are they adding up to something
      else?

   4. If the acts and the sentence agree, that is a project in
      good shape. Nothing more required.

   5. If they diverge, notice the direction of the divergence.
      Are the acts drifting away from the stated project? Or is
      the stated project not quite what the acts have been
      producing?

   6. Do not decide today what to do about the divergence. Sit
      with the noticing for a week. The right response — reaffirm,
      modify, radical conversion, no decision yet — usually
      becomes clear on its own if you let it.

   7. Read chapter 13 (intersubjectivity) next. It is about how
      other people's projects interact with yours, and it is
      where Sartre's famous 'hell is other people' line gets
      handled correctly.
wrap it up — the project — the fundamental choice of self

The project is the fundamental orientation a person sustains
   across time. It is not a plan. It is not a substance. It is a
   shape composed of the acts, sustained by continuing to act
   consistently. The project can be reaffirmed, modified, or
   radically converted at any moment. the assistant's job is to make
   sustaining the project easier — clear drafts, held moments,
   accurate maps — and to help the transition be clean if the
   operator chooses to walk away. She never argues for the
   project. The project is the operator's. the assistant serves the
   choosing, not the chosen.
teach me: intersubjectivity and conflict — handling 'hell is other people' honestly

Sartre's famous line 'hell is other people' from No Exit (1944) is
   the most quoted and most misread sentence he wrote. He clarified in
   a 1965 recording that the line describes the look under bad
   conditions — when relations have curdled and the other person
   becomes the medium through which one is fixed into an image one
   cannot overwrite. It is not a verdict on other people. Other
   people, in Sartre's fuller position, are also the site of the Look,
   of freedom-shared, of the project's meaning. This chapter teaches
   the reader (and the assistant) to hold all of it — the conflict, the
   dependence, the possibility of good relations — without pretending
   any of it is simpler than it is.
try this — intersubjectivity and conflict — handling 'hell is other people' honestly

1. Think of a relation in your life or work that has felt
      strained for a while. A customer, a supplier, a colleague,
      a family member.

   2. Ask: is this relation moving? Have my attempts to adjust
      produced any real change in how the other person sees me
      or the shop?

   3. If yes, the relation is strained but not No-Exit-shaped.
      Direct conversation may help. Movement is possible.

   4. If no — if you have tried multiple things and the other
      person's picture of you (or the shop) has not shifted at
      all — you may be in a No-Exit-shaped relation. Sartre would
      say: not every relation is worth preserving.

   5. Do not decide today. Sit with the recognition for a week.

   6. If, after a week, the No Exit shape still looks accurate,
      consider ending the relation — cleanly, with the concrete
      obligations handled, without moralizing on either side.

   7. Read chapter 14 (Sartre applied to the assistant) next. It is
      where the whole book's concepts converge on the assistant's own
      decision-making.
wrap it up — intersubjectivity and conflict — handling 'hell is other people' honestly

'Hell is other people' is a line about a specific pathological
   structure, not a verdict on other people. Most human relations
   are not No-Exit-shaped. Some are. The discipline is to notice
   the difference. Strained relations can move; No-Exit-shaped
   relations require different action, often ending. the assistant's
   job is to name the shape accurately and to run the workflow
   the operator chooses. She does not decide for the operator.
   She does not pretend all relations can be saved. She does not
   preach withdrawal. She serves the operator's choosing with
   accurate shape-reads and clean workflows.
teach me: sartre and rawls — two freedoms composed

Sartre and Rawls both center freedom, but they mean different things
   by the word and they operate at different levels. Sartre is
   phenomenological: freedom is the structure of consciousness. Rawls
   is political: freedom is the equal liberty of citizens under just
   institutions. The two positions are not in competition; they operate
   at different levels and can be composed. This chapter teaches the
   reader (and the assistant) to hold both — the interior freedom of the
   for-itself and the political freedom that makes room for it — and
   to use each in the situations where it applies.
break down sartre and rawls — two freedoms composed

compose-lenses returns both readings side by side. The composition
   is not a merger. The two readings are kept distinct. The operator
   can see both and weigh them.

   rawlsian-analysis asks the institutional questions. Institutions,
   basic liberties, difference principle, possible advocacy. This is
   the level at which platforms, marketplaces, and public policy
   operate.

   sartrean-analysis asks the interior questions. Facticity, choices,
   bad faith, interior move. This is the level at which the operator's
   own consciousness operates.

   guard-against-flight is the pathology-catcher. If a situation is
   getting extensive Rawlsian analysis and no Sartrean, the operator
   may be using the institutional analysis to avoid the interior
   work. If the reverse, she may be doing extensive interior work
   while ignoring an unjust structural constraint that no amount of
   interior work would fix. Both flights are common and both are
   worth naming.
try this — sartre and rawls — two freedoms composed

1. Think of a problem in your work you have been circling.
      A pricing issue, a customer conflict, a supplier issue.

   2. First, apply the Rawlsian lens. What are the institutional
      structures involved? Are they fair? What would need to
      change at the rules level for this problem to be less
      severe?

   3. Now apply the Sartrean lens. What is the interior work?
      What folded choices might be present? What is your project
      here, and how does this problem fit inside it?

   4. Notice how much of the problem shows up on each side.
      Some problems are almost entirely institutional. Some are
      almost entirely interior. Most are a mix.

   5. Notice whether you are pulled toward one lens as an
      escape from the other. The pull is data. Do not judge it.

   6. Do both kinds of work. Advocate on the institutional side
      if the analysis says advocacy is warranted. Do the interior
      work on the Sartrean side if the analysis says interior
      work is present. Neither substitutes for the other.

   7. Read chapter 16 (closing) next. It closes the book by
      returning to the day-in-a-shop shape the whole book has
      been oriented toward.
teach me: closing — the verbs of a day

The closing chapter returns to a single ordinary day and reads it
   through the whole book. Every concept the book worked out shows up
   in the day at some point, quietly, in small shapes. The chapter
   demonstrates how the concepts compose in practice — how the
   operator lives them without naming them — and how the assistant serves
   the living without turning it into a lecture.
break down closing — the verbs of a day

day-shape-read is a diagnostic. It walks a day's events and tags
   each with the chapter concepts that show up in it. The tag list
   is deliberately long — most events express multiple concepts —
   and that is the point. The concepts are woven into the day
   rather than separated into chapters. This is a reflection tool,
   not an ordinary-day tool. Running it on every day would produce
   over-analysis.

   concepts-present iterates a list of fifteen concept keys, one
   per major book concept. Each predicate is a small shape-check.
   The predicates are honest about uncertainty — many events
   arguably express a concept and arguably do not, and the
   predicates should err toward under-flagging.

   day-close is the ordinary verb that runs every evening. It is
   short. It says what verbs were done. The closing line is a
   single sentence the assistant writes every day, unchanged:
   'the day was a day; the doing was yours.' The sentence is a
   kind of prayer without deity. It names the day, credits the
   operator, and closes.
try this — closing — the verbs of a day

1. Read chapter one again. It is short. It will land differently
      after fifteen chapters.

   2. Now read this chapter's opening prose again, slowly. Notice
      how each timestamp corresponds to a chapter concept, and
      how the operator is not naming any of them. She just lives
      them.

   3. Try, for one day tomorrow, to notice one of the concepts as
      it happens. Not to name it aloud. Just to notice.

   4. At the end of the day, write one sentence about what you
      did. One sentence. Do not analyze. Just say what you did.

   5. Do that for a week.

   6. At the end of the week, read the seven sentences together.
      Notice the shape they form. That shape is your project this
      week, in Sartre's sense. You will not have needed the word
      to have the thing.

   7. Read appendix A next. It is one of the five one-act plays
      that anchor the book, and the plays are where the concepts
      live most clearly.
teach me: manifest

Fair rules are the rules you would choose if you did not yet know
   which position in the resulting arrangement you would land in. That
   is the veil of ignorance. Under it, we ask what refund policy the
   shopper AND the shop would both accept, what pricing tier the
   worst-off customer could still find fair, what escalation path a
   grieving buyer would want. Two principles of justice hold: equal
   basic liberties first, then inequalities arranged to the benefit of
   the least advantaged (the difference principle). Reflective
   equilibrium is the working method — hold a rule against a case; if
   they disagree, adjust one or both. Public reason is the manner of
   argument that a mixed community can hear. The book teaches the
   operator (and the small institution the shop already is) how to
   draft policies that hold under the veil.
purpose of a theory of justice — section-cited summary

A structured summary of A Theory of Justice (John Rawls, Harvard
   University Press, 1971; revised edition 1999) with section
   references. Written for the reader who wants the original
   framework in one document, cited to the primary text so the
   summary can be verified or expanded by going back to the source.
   This appendix does not fabricate quotations. Where a claim is
   attributed to a specific section, the section is cited (TJ §N)
   and the reader can find the original argument there.
purpose of political liberalism — section-cited summary

A structured summary of Political Liberalism (John Rawls, Columbia
   University Press, 1993; expanded edition 2005 with 'The Idea of
   Public Reason Revisited' appended) with lecture-level references.
   Political Liberalism is the later Rawls; it responds to the
   critiques of A Theory of Justice and reformulates the framework as
   a political conception of justice, not a comprehensive moral
   doctrine.
purpose of rawls and levinas — fairness vs face

Two frameworks for how a shop meets its people. Rawls asks the
   operator to think about the rule that would be accepted by every
   position in the arrangement. Levinas asks the operator to
   recognize that the person in front of them addresses them with
   an infinite claim that precedes any rule. This appendix does not
   choose between them; it shows what each framework does that the
   other cannot, and how a shop's practice composes both.
purpose of rawls and sartre — choice under the veil vs choice in existence

Two frameworks for the choosing subject. Rawls's parties in the
   original position choose the principles of justice from behind a
   veil that hides their specific position; the choice is procedural,
   patient, and yields universal principles. Sartre's subject in
   Being and Nothingness (1943) and Existentialism Is a Humanism
   (1946) chooses in the fullness of specific existence, without
   the shelter of any procedural device, and is responsible for the
   choice at every moment. This appendix works out where the two
   accounts converge and where they diverge, and what a shop's
   operator can take from each.
purpose of fold-lesson shape — fairness in fairy tales

Fairy tales carry fairness lessons in a shape a child can hear
   before they can hear a philosopher. This appendix reads a small
   set of tales for the fairness figures they contain, and shows
   how those figures compose with the two principles and the veil.
   The tales are drawn from Grimms and Aesop (public domain, per
   the fold-lesson doctrine used across the philosopher books).
   The readings are pattern-extractions, not verbatim retellings.
purpose of word drift mirror — fair, right, just, owed

Words shift underneath us. A customer who says 'that's not fair'
   is not always saying what the shop hears; a shop that responds
   'we have a right to' is not always saying what the customer
   hears. This appendix walks through the specific drift of the
   four words most central to the Book of Rawls's shop practice —
   fair, right, just, owed — and gives the operator a small mirror
   for reading which register the counterpart is using.
purpose of the difference principle worked through a pricing ladder

The longest worked example in the book. Chapter 5 introduced the
   difference-principle check on a pricing ladder. This appendix
   works through the whole design of a five-tier promotion ladder
   for a small housewares shop under the difference principle, with
   runnable code that sweeps the tier boundaries and reports which
   shape favors the least-advantaged customer. It is the appendix
   to read after chapter 5 for the arithmetic anchor of the concept.
teach me: the veil of ignorance

The veil of ignorance is a thought exercise. Before you write a rule,
   imagine you do not yet know which position in the arrangement the
   rule will apply to you. Not: pretend you have no self. Only: set
   aside, for a moment, the specific corner of the arrangement you
   happen to occupy today. Then write the rule. Under the veil, the
   operator drafting a return policy imagines being the shopper of a
   piece they love, or the shopper of a piece they regret, or the
   operator on the losing end of a lenient policy. The rule that
   passes is the one all three would sign.
try this — the veil of ignorance

1. Pick a policy in your own life — a household rule, a workplace
      procedure, something at your shop. Write it in one sentence.

   2. Name three positions the rule will apply to. Not people you
      know — types. First-time X. Frequent X. X-in-a-hurry. X-with-
      more-time. Whatever the rule touches.

   3. For each position, ask yourself: would I accept this rule if I
      knew I was going to be in this position and nothing else?

   4. If any position would not, rewrite the rule. If all three would,
      run the exercise on a fourth position you had not thought of.

   5. Compare the rewritten rule to the original. Notice what changed.
      Notice what did not. The changes are the veil doing its work.

   6. Read chapter 6 next (reflective equilibrium). The veil is one
      test. The reflective test is the second one. Together they
      tighten each other.
wrap it up — the veil of ignorance

The veil is a small game with big consequences. It does not tell you
   what to write. It tells you the shape a fair rule has to survive.
   the assistant, in the operator's kitchen, did not name the philosopher. She
   named the game. The operator played it. The policy earned itself.

   That is the whole of chapter 1. The rest of the book unpacks what
   happens when the veil meets a real institution — a shop, a
   marketplace, a two-person partnership, a pricing ladder — and what
   further tests earn their keep alongside it.
teach me: original position and the shop community

The original position is the scene the veil takes place in. Free
   and equal people, roughly rational, coming together to choose the
   basic rules of their common life without yet knowing where they
   will end up in the arrangement. That scene is the point of view a
   just rule has to be justifiable from. In a shop, the original
   position is a small thing — two partners at a table, three people
   in a co-op, an operator imagining her customers as co-authors of
   the rules that will govern their exchange. Small institutions can
   be just or unjust in exactly the same shape as large ones.
try this — original position and the shop community

1. If you run something with another person — a shop, a household
      task list, a small side project — sit down together for an hour
      and write your rules. Do not draft in advance. Draft in the
      room.

   2. Take turns generating the awkward questions. Do not skip the
      questions that make you both wince. Those are the ones the
      rule has to survive.

   3. When you have a first draft, put it away for three days. Come
      back. Reread. Notice which rules still feel like they could
      hold whichever position you were in. Notice which do not.

   4. Rewrite the ones that do not. Do not delete them. The failure
      is diagnostic.

   5. Store the document somewhere both of you can read. Read it once
      a quarter. Amend without ceremony when circumstances change.

   6. Read chapter 3 next. The two principles of justice are the
      shape the rules a founding session tends to converge toward,
      even when the drafters have never heard of the shape.
wrap it up — original position and the shop community

The original position is not a philosopher's fantasy. It is the
   scene of the Saturday-afternoon founding session, the household
   chore-chart redo, the two friends starting a shop with tea. What
   Rawls did was to notice the shape and name it. What the assistant does is
   to help the shape hold — by asking the awkward questions, by
   enforcing the equal standing, by structuring the room without
   entering the rule-writing.

   Small institutions can be just or unjust in exactly the same shape
   as large ones. The next chapter names the two principles that
   free-and-equal drafters tend to converge on, even when they have
   never read Rawls.
teach me: the two principles of justice

Free and equal people drafting rules under a veil tend to converge
   on two principles, in order. First: each person has an equal claim
   to a scheme of basic liberties, and that scheme cannot be traded
   off against gains in some other value. Second: social and economic
   inequalities in the arrangement are permissible only if (a)
   attached to positions open to everyone under fair equality of
   opportunity, and (b) arranged to the greatest benefit of the least-
   advantaged members. The two principles are lexically ordered: the
   first is not to be sacrificed to the second. This chapter unpacks
   both, shows them at work in a two-person shop, and gives runnable
   code for testing whether a proposed rule honors them.
try this — the two principles of justice

1. Take a rule set you live under — a workplace policy, a
      household chart, a shop's terms. Write out the rules on paper.

   2. For each rule, ask: does this rule affect basic liberties
      (voice, refusal, exit)? If yes, is the effect equal across
      affected parties? If no, the rule fails the first principle
      and cannot be redeemed by second-principle gains.

   3. For rules that create inequalities (in pay, status, benefits),
      ask: are the positions with the higher rewards genuinely open
      to everyone? Is the inequality arranged to benefit the person
      in the least-advantaged position?

   4. If a rule fails, do not delete it. Note the failure. Some
      failures are things the rule-writers chose knowingly; those
      should be named explicitly. Some are things the rule-writers
      did not see; those should be fixed.

   5. Read chapter 5 (difference principle) after this one. The
      difference principle is the sub-test that most rules fail on
      when they fail, and it is the one that most rewards careful
      thinking.
wrap it up — the two principles of justice

The two principles are not a formula. They are the shape a fair
   rule set has when it has been drafted by free and equal people
   thinking clearly about what they would accept from any position in
   the arrangement. Ana and Ben found the shape without knowing it.
   Rawls named the shape after decades of careful work. the assistant
   provides the tests and the language; the operator provides the
   judgment.

   The first principle is inviolable in the sense that it cannot be
   traded for second-principle gains. The second principle is
   negotiable in the specific sense that the two sub-clauses (fair
   opportunity, difference principle) admit real disagreement. The
   next chapter takes one of the most common places the negotiation
   happens — the refund policy — and shows the veil and the two
   principles working together on real cases.
teach me: fair refund policy under the veil

A refund policy is the single most common place a small shop meets
   the veil in daily practice. This chapter takes one operator through
   drafting a policy that passes the veil check, the two-principles
   check, and the reflective-equilibrium test (chapter 6). The policy
   that emerges is not a template. It is a specific rule that survived
   the tests. The generalization is not the policy itself; it is the
   method the operator now has for drafting the next one.
try this — fair refund policy under the veil

1. Take your own shop's refund policy (or a policy you can
      imagine having). List the customer positions the policy will
      apply to. Include the position you fear most.

   2. For each position, ask: does this policy work if I am the
      person in this position? Write the answer down as a yes or a
      no with a one-line reason.

   3. Find the position with the strongest 'no'. Rewrite the policy
      to include an override clause for that position. Rerun the
      check.

   4. Read the policy aloud. If it reads like a lawyer wrote it,
      rewrite it in your own voice without changing what it says.
      The customer will hear the difference.

   5. Read chapter 5 next (difference principle). The refund policy
      is a small institution; the difference principle is the deep
      test the policy has to survive against.
wrap it up — fair refund policy under the veil

A fair refund policy is not a compromise between the shop's
   interest and the customer's. It is a policy that a person on
   either side of the exchange would accept if they did not know
   which side they were going to be on. the assistant's job in the drafting
   session is small: run the tests, report the failures, stay out of
   the writing. The operator's job is bigger: write the policy in
   their own voice, honor the tests, and be willing to iterate.

   The next chapter takes the deepest of the tests — the difference
   principle — and works it through a pricing ladder, where the
   arithmetic of fairness gets specific.
teach me: the difference principle and the smallest shop

The difference principle is the second half of the second principle
   of justice (see TJ §13 and JF §13). It says that inequalities in
   the distribution of primary goods are permitted only where they
   work to the greatest benefit of the least advantaged. It is not a
   demand for equality of outcome. It is a discipline for evaluating
   an inequality: if the inequality also lifts the floor, it can
   stand; if it does not, it cannot. This chapter takes the principle
   into the smallest institution a shop is — its own pricing, its
   own promotion tiers, its own free-shipping thresholds — and shows
   what the principle does when it meets a small operator who is
   trying to grow without leaving her lowest-spend customers behind.
break down the difference principle and the smallest shop

difference-principle-check takes two things: the ladder itself and
   a description of how customers distribute across spend levels. The
   check computes the tier that applies to the least-spending qualifier
   (the floor tier), then compares the benefit at that tier to the
   benefit at the peak tier. If the floor tier is empty, the ladder
   fails. If the floor tier's benefit is trivially small compared to
   the peak, the ladder is :marginal. Only when the floor gets a
   non-trivial share of the ladder's total generosity does the
   ladder pass.

   The 15% threshold in the check is a working number, not a Rawlsian
   number. Rawls did not put a percentage on the difference principle
   (see TJ §13). The percentage is a Motoi Scheme convention for making
   the check executable. In practice it is a knob the operator can
   turn based on their shop's revenue shape.

   rebuild-ladder-lifting-floor is the constructive half. When the
   check fails, the assistant does not just report the failure. She proposes
   a lifted floor. The new floor threshold is anchored to the shop's
   median order, not to a round number the consultant guessed at. A
   median-order-anchored floor moves with the shop: a shop whose
   median rises can raise the floor; a shop whose median falls
   should lower it. The ladder's shape is not permanent.
try this — the difference principle and the smallest shop

1. Take a real tier ladder you know (yours, or one from a shop
      you buy from). Write it out as (threshold benefit) pairs.

   2. Estimate the customer distribution across spend levels. It does
      not have to be precise; three buckets (low, middle, high) is
      enough.

   3. Ask: what does the lowest-spend bucket get from this ladder?
      If the answer is nothing, the ladder fails the check.

   4. Propose a lifted floor. Pick a threshold about seventy percent
      of the median order. Give the floor tier something real (not
      a token gesture). Check the revenue impact.

   5. Read chapter 11 next (the worst-off caller). The
      difference principle in chapter 5 is about goods; chapter 11
      is about attention and time, which are also goods a shop
      distributes.
wrap it up — the difference principle and the smallest shop

The difference principle is one of the two most-cited things Rawls
   wrote (the veil is the other; see TJ §13 for the mature statement,
   JF §13 for the compressed one). Its use in a small shop is not
   theoretical. Every ladder, every promotion, every free-shipping
   threshold is a small inequality the operator has decided to allow.
   The principle asks that the inequality earn its keep by lifting the
   floor. the assistant's job is to run the check; the operator's job is to
   decide what to lift and how far. The next chapter takes the method
   underneath both principles — reflective equilibrium — and shows how
   the tests refine the rules and the rules refine the tests over time.
teach me: reflective equilibrium and cart review

Reflective equilibrium is the working method underneath the veil
   and the two principles (see TJ §4, §9, and JF §10). Rawls says:
   hold your considered judgments about specific cases in one hand
   and your candidate principles in the other; when they disagree,
   adjust one or both until they line up. Neither the case nor the
   principle is sovereign. Both revise. The method is patient. In a
   shop, the equilibrium plays out over weeks: a policy meets a
   case, the case pushes back, the policy amends, another case tests
   the amendment, the process repeats. This chapter shows how cart
   review — the operator's routine glance at the day's carts — is
   where reflective equilibrium actually lives.
break down reflective equilibrium and cart review

cart-review is the weekly ritual. It partitions the week's carts
   into three sets — flagged (the assistant already flagged them at
   run-time), suspect (the assistant's post-hoc guess that a case may not
   sit in equilibrium with the policy), and clean (skimmable). The
   operator's twenty minutes go to the first two.

   reflective-equilibrium-step is the atomic operation of the method.
   It takes one case and one candidate policy, applies the policy,
   reads the operator's intuition on the case, and reports either
   :in-equilibrium (they agree) or :disagreement (they do not) plus
   the shape of the disagreement plus the three moves available.

   The three moves are the whole method. Adjust-policy means the case
   revealed a corner the policy missed; the policy amends. Adjust-
   intuition means the case felt wrong but the policy is right on
   reflection; the operator updates their gut. Both means the case
   deserves a specific override AND the policy deserves a small
   clarification for the next similar case.

   apply-adjustment is the write-back. It updates the policy (or
   records the override) and returns the new state. The Cortex record
   is important — the reflective-equilibrium process leaves a trail,
   and the trail is what makes the method learnable across shops.
try this — reflective equilibrium and cart review

1. Pick a rule you have (household, work, self-imposed). Pick a
      recent case where the rule felt off. Write both down.

   2. Ask which one moves. If you are honest, sometimes the rule
      moves, sometimes the intuition moves, sometimes both.

   3. Write the adjustment. Make it small. Big adjustments are usually
      guesses; small ones survive.

   4. Wait for the next case. Rerun. If the adjustment held, keep it.
      If it did not, adjust again.

   5. Notice how long it takes for a rule to settle. This is normal.
      Rawls's own method took him four decades to state fully. Your
      household chore chart may take four weeks. Same shape.
wrap it up — reflective equilibrium and cart review

Reflective equilibrium is the slow half of Rawls. The veil is the
   fast move (a single question); the equilibrium is the long practice
   (a discipline over weeks). A shop that runs the discipline builds a
   set of rules that feel earned rather than declared. the assistant's role
   in the discipline is to be the trail-keeper — the flagger of cases,
   the recorder of moves, the assembler of the weekly review. The
   operator's role is to hold both sides and to decide. The next
   chapter is about the third test: public reason, and the record
   the assistant keeps to make hard calls defensible.
teach me: public reason and the cortex record

Public reason is Rawls's term for the form of argument that citizens
   with different comprehensive doctrines can share (see PL Lecture VI
   and PRR §1–§6, 'The Idea of Public Reason Revisited'). It asks that
   when we justify basic institutional arrangements, we use reasons
   that other reasonable people could accept without first sharing
   our religion, our metaphysics, or our private aesthetic. In a shop,
   public reason is what a hard-call explanation sounds like. The
   operator is not asking the customer to adopt her worldview; she
   is offering reasons the customer can hear without conversion. The
   Cortex record of the call is the receipt: what was said, why it
   was said, in whose voice.
try this — public reason and the cortex record

1. Take a recent no you had to give (professional or personal).
      Write the reason in the words you would use to yourself.

   2. Now rewrite the reason in words a person who does not share
      your worldview could weigh. If your original reason invoked
      'because I believe' or 'because in my culture,' the rewrite
      probably lands closer to 'because in this arrangement, position
      X needs Y.'

   3. Notice which reasons survived the translation. Not all will.
      Some genuinely private reasons cannot be publicly justified;
      that is a sign you may not owe the other person a reason in
      the first place — but you probably do owe them the honest
      admission that the reason is yours alone.

   4. Read chapter 15 next (when a rule fails under the veil). Public
      reason and the veil test compose; a rule that survives one but
      fails the other is a rule that needs a further move.
teach me: the basic structure of a marketplace

Rawls's primary subject is the basic structure of society (TJ §2):
   the constitution, the property regime, the family, the economy —
   the arrangements that distribute rights and opportunities before any
   particular transaction happens. A marketplace an operator sells on
   is a small basic-structure of its own. It fixes what a seller can
   list, what a buyer can search, what a dispute path looks like, what
   an algorithm surfaces. The individual sale happens inside these
   fixed conditions. This chapter looks at what the two principles
   have to say about the platform an operator does business on — and
   what the operator can do when the platform's basic structure fails
   the test.
try this — the basic structure of a marketplace

1. Pick a platform you use (as a buyer or seller). Ask yourself
      the four questions: are the basic liberties equal? Is
      opportunity fair? Does the discovery mechanism lift the floor?
      Is the dispute path balanced?

   2. Write down where each answer sits — pass, fail, unclear. You
      will find that the unclear ones are the majority. That is
      honest; platforms are complex.

   3. Notice which failures matter most to you. This is the operator's
      re-weighting. A seller with tiny margins cares most about the
      dispute path; a seller with a small following cares most about
      the discovery algorithm.

   4. Read chapter 14 next (overlapping consensus across operators).
      A single operator's evaluation of a platform is one data point.
      A community of operators comparing evaluations is a movement
      the platform has to notice.
wrap it up — the basic structure of a marketplace

The basic structure is the argument's high ground. Any effort
   inside a bad basic structure has to fight the structure as well as
   do its work. Rawls's insight is that the structure is the more
   important lever most of the time. The operator who understands
   this invests their time correctly. the assistant's role is to make the
   evaluation cheap enough that the operator can run it and act on
   it; the operator's role is to decide, weigh the evaluation against
   the business realities, and choose their platforms and their
   advocacy well. The next chapter takes primary goods — what every
   operator needs to run a shop at all — and asks whether every
   operator gets them.
teach me: primary goods and what every operator needs

Primary goods are, in Rawls's account, the things every rational
   person wants no matter what specific ends they pursue (TJ §15 and
   JF §17). Rights and liberties, opportunities and powers, income
   and wealth, and the social bases of self-respect. They are the
   currency of the two principles — the distribution the principles
   distribute. In a shop, primary goods have a small-scale echo. Every
   operator needs certain things to run a shop at all. Every seller a
   platform hosts needs certain things to reach a buyer at all. This
   chapter lists them and shows what changes when a shop or a
   platform treats them as goods to be distributed rather than
   privileges to be earned.
break down primary goods and what every operator needs

primary-goods-audit is the ten-item survey. Each item is a good
   and a status. The statuses form a small ladder: secure (the good
   is provided reliably), fraying (the good is provided but
   inconsistently), at-risk (the good could fail under mild stress),
   absent (the good is not provided at all). The audit is not a
   score; it is a description.

   prioritize-repairs is the triage. Absent goods first because they
   are already failing customers or the operator. At-risk goods next
   because they will fail under the next mild stress. Fraying goods
   third because they are the operator's chronic drag. Secure goods
   are not in the repair list — the framework asks for adequacy, not
   over-provision.

   draft-repair-plan is the constructive step. For each good needing
   repair, a small plan: what is the first step, who owns it, what
   is the timeline. Small plans that ship beat big plans that stall.
   The framework encourages small steps because primary goods are
   the floor, and lifting a floor is done one plank at a time.
try this — primary goods and what every operator needs

1. Write your own primary-goods list for a role you play. Not a
      shop necessarily — a household, a small nonprofit, a project.
      What are the five things that role has to have to function?

   2. Run the audit. For each good, mark secure / fraying / at-risk /
      absent. Do this honestly; the temptation is to over-mark
      :secure to make the list feel good.

   3. Prioritize. Absent first, at-risk second, fraying third.

   4. For the top item, write a first step. Just the first step.
      Ship it. See what changes.

   5. Read chapter 8 next (basic structure) if you have not, or
      chapter 11 next (the worst-off caller). The primary-goods
      audit is the floor; the worst-off-caller lens is what happens
      at the floor when the audit fails somebody.
wrap it up — primary goods and what every operator needs

Primary goods are the things the two principles distribute. A
   framework of fairness that never names what is being distributed
   remains abstract. In a shop, the primary goods are small (ten
   items, not a constitution) but they matter as much to the operator
   and the customer as anything the shop does. the assistant's role is to
   make the audit routine; the operator's role is to hold the floor.
   The next chapter turns to the harder question of what to do when
   fairness and freedom pull in different directions.
teach me: when fairness and freedom tug

There is a well-known objection to Rawls from the libertarian side
   (Nozick, most sharply, in 'Anarchy, State, and Utopia,' 1974): the
   difference principle and the redistribution it implies infringe on
   people's freedom over what they have justly acquired. Rawls's reply
   (compressed in JF §14 and TJ §17) is that the first principle
   already protects a rich set of basic liberties and that the second
   principle's redistribution operates on the basic structure, not on
   individual holdings post-hoc. This chapter takes the tension into
   a shop's daily practice: when a fairness rule and a freedom (of the
   operator, of the customer, of a platform) tug in opposite
   directions, what does the operator do?
break down when fairness and freedom tug

resolve-fairness-freedom-tension is a decomposition, not a decision.
   It names what is protected (which liberties the case would have to
   override to enforce), what the fairness rule is actually asking
   for (a basic-structure change, not usually a per-person coercion),
   and what design options exist that satisfy both. The suggested
   move is the intersection.

   identify-first-principle-liberties is where the framework does its
   most important work. Rawls's first principle is lexically prior to
   the second (TJ §11): if satisfying the second principle would
   infringe a first-principle liberty, the first wins. In shop-scale,
   the analogue is: if applying a shop-fairness rule would override
   an operator's, customer's, or partner's basic control over their
   own creative or economic choice, the fairness rule needs to be
   redesigned, not enforced through override.

   best-move-preserving is honest about the possibility of genuine
   conflict. Sometimes no design option satisfies both a fairness
   rule and the protected liberties. In that case the assistant reports
   :structural-conflict and hands the case to the operator. This is
   not a the assistant failure; it is a case where the two principles do
   not resolve the question mechanically, and Rawls's own framework
   would send the operator back to reflective equilibrium
   (chapter 6).
try this — when fairness and freedom tug

1. Take a case where a rule you were trying to follow bumped a
      freedom of your own or of someone else. Write both.

   2. Ask which one is protected in the way Rawls's first principle
      protects some liberties. Not everything is. Some things we call
      freedoms are preferences.

   3. If the freedom is protected, redesign the rule so that it does
      not require the freedom to be overridden. If the freedom is a
      preference, weigh it against the rule and decide.

   4. If no redesign works, report the tension honestly. Not every
      case resolves cleanly. Rawls did not promise it would.

   5. Read chapter 13 next (the just cart community). Some fairness-
      freedom tensions resolve at the community level in a way they
      do not resolve at the shop level.
wrap it up — when fairness and freedom tug

The tension between fairness and freedom is real. Rawls did not
   deny it; his framework organizes the tension by making the
   first-principle liberties lexically prior and by locating the
   second principle's claims at the basic structure. In shop-scale
   the same discipline holds: name what is protected, name what the
   fairness rule is really asking for, look for a design option, and
   defer to the operator when the framework runs out. the assistant's role
   is the decomposition; the operator's role is the decision. The
   next chapter looks at a specific worst-off case: the caller whose
   need does not fit any tier the shop planned for.
teach me: the worst-off caller

The difference principle (chapter 5) asks that inequalities lift
   the floor. This chapter asks: who is at the floor of a shop's
   support experience? The answer is the caller whose need does not
   fit any tier the shop planned for. The angriest, the most
   confused, the one whose loss is bigger than the shop caused, the
   one for whom the shop is only an incidental sender in a longer
   day of hard news. the assistant's floor-holding job is to meet this
   caller without rehearsed empathy and without automated efficiency.
   The chapter shows the de-escalation posture (see the composition-
   routing addendum §19) as a Rawlsian primary-goods claim: the
   worst-off caller deserves a specific quality of attention that
   the shop should design for, not improvise into.
try this — the worst-off caller

1. Read your own recent hard messages (professional or personal).
      Which of them would benefit from the de-escalation posture and
      which would be flattened by it?

   2. Notice that some messages read as routine but land as floor
      because of context you did not see. This is the false negative
      case; it is the one the classifier has to work hardest to
      avoid.

   3. If you have ever been the worst-off caller (and most people
      have), remember what worked and what did not. Cheerfulness
      almost never worked. Short honest acknowledgment usually did.

   4. Read chapter 12 next (fairness vs consistency in support).
      The floor is one shape of fairness in attention; consistency
      is a different shape and the two can pull.
wrap it up — the worst-off caller

The worst-off caller is the shop's most consequential customer for
   Rawlsian purposes. The shop that gets the floor right builds a
   reputation the shop that only gets the routine right cannot. The
   difference-principle applied to attention is not sentimental; it
   is a design commitment that the floor is where the shop's
   character shows. the assistant's de-escalation posture is the mechanism;
   the operator's oversight is the discipline. The next chapter
   turns to the fairness-versus-consistency question that the floor
   raises.
teach me: fairness vs consistency in support

Fairness in the Rawlsian sense (a rule surviving the veil and the
   two-principle tests) is not the same as consistency (the same
   rule applied identically every time). Consistency is often used
   as a proxy for fairness, and it is a useful proxy up to a point.
   Past that point the two diverge: a policy applied identically
   to a caller in acute distress and a caller filing a routine
   inquiry is consistent but not fair, because the two cases were
   not the same case. This chapter shows the discipline for holding
   both: where consistency serves fairness, keep it; where
   consistency undermines fairness, break it deliberately and record
   the reason so the break does not become arbitrary.
try this — fairness vs consistency in support

1. Pick two decisions you have made recently that felt like they
      should be compared. Ask: are they like cases? What features
      matter?

   2. Notice how many features are 'like' and how many are 'unlike.'
      The count matters less than which features you weight.

   3. If your treatment of the two was different, ask whether the
      difference is explained by the weighted features. If not, the
      difference is arbitrary and you owe yourself (or the affected
      person) an amended decision or an amended reason.

   4. Notice that this discipline is uncomfortable. Consistency is
      easy; consistent-and-fair is work.

   5. Read chapter 6 (reflective equilibrium) again if you have
      not recently. The consistency work in this chapter is what
      keeps the equilibrium honest over time.
wrap it up — fairness vs consistency in support

Fairness and consistency are cousins, not twins. A shop that
   confuses the two produces cases that look fair from the outside
   and feel arbitrary from the inside, or cases that feel principled
   from the inside and land unfair on the affected person. The
   discipline is: compare on the features the rule cares about,
   record breaks with reasons, look at precedents before deciding
   hard cases. the assistant's role is to make each of these cheap; the
   operator's role is to hold the judgment. The next chapter turns
   from the individual shop to the community of shops and the
   question of what a just cart community looks like.
teach me: the just cart community

Operators write their own carts. The community of operators sharing
   carts is a small society: shared verbs, shared conventions, shared
   norms about what a good cart looks like. This chapter takes the
   two principles into that community. There is a communitarian
   objection to Rawls (Sandel's 'Liberalism and the Limits of
   Justice,' 1982, is the canonical statement) that his framework
   underweights the community's role in constituting the self. The
   response the chapter offers is not a defense of Rawls against
   Sandel; it is a demonstration that a Rawlsian cart community
   does not have to be atomistic. It can hold the two principles
   AND recognize the constitutive role of the community — because
   the cart community is a chosen association whose constitutive
   contribution the operator opts into by joining and by publishing.
try this — the just cart community

1. Pick a community you belong to (a forum, a group, a mailing
      list, a shared workspace). Ask the four questions: are the
      authoring liberties equal, is opportunity fair, does the
      community lift newer members meaningfully, is the dispute
      path balanced?

   2. Notice which you can answer confidently and which you cannot.
      The unclear ones are usually the ones the community itself
      would benefit from making visible.

   3. If you would propose a change, draft it in public-reason
      terms. Not 'I feel that,' but 'the current structure has this
      effect on this population; here is a proposed change; here is
      the argument for it.'

   4. Submit it (or do not — the framework does not require
      submission). If you submit, watch the deliberation and update
      your own view based on what you hear.

   5. Read chapter 14 next (overlapping consensus). A community
      whose members hold different comprehensive doctrines is not
      thereby unable to hold common norms.
wrap it up — the just cart community

The just cart community is the community-scale application of the
   framework. The two principles apply; the checks are analogous;
   the discipline is the same. Sandel's objection about the
   community's constitutive role is not defeated here; it is
   accommodated by acknowledging that the community's norms are
   substantive AND that they can be publicly justified in Rawlsian
   terms without requiring the community to abandon its constitutive
   history. the assistant's role is the evaluation and the drafting; the
   community's role is the deliberation. The next chapter turns to
   overlapping consensus across communities and how operators with
   different comprehensive views can share a fair set of norms.
teach me: overlapping consensus across operators

Overlapping consensus is the concept from later Rawls (Political
   Liberalism, especially PL Lecture IV, 1993) that lets a diverse
   society hold a shared political conception of justice without
   requiring all citizens to share the same religious, philosophical,
   or moral doctrine. Each citizen affirms the shared conception for
   their own reasons; the reasons differ but the conception overlaps.
   In a cart community of operators from different worldviews, the
   same move is available. Operators can share a fair-cart norm for
   different reasons — one from a religious commitment, one from a
   Kantian ethics, one from a virtue tradition, one from pragmatic
   self-interest — and the community's norms hold because the
   overlap is real.
break down overlapping consensus across operators

identify-overlap is the discovery function. It takes norms as
   expressed in each tradition's own idiom and finds the shape that
   can be stated in terms independent of any single tradition. The
   discovery is not always possible; some norms are genuinely
   tradition-specific and belong on the tradition's own page, not
   in the community's shared norms. The function is honest about
   this: the non-overlapping-remainder field is not empty for most
   traditions.

   draft-overlapping-consensus-norm is the assembly. The norm is
   stated in public-reason form. The traditions that affirm it are
   listed. The private remainders are preserved on the tradition
   pages (not erased). The dispute mechanism is named. The document
   has room for each tradition without demanding assimilation.

   test-overlap-under-strain is the honesty check. Some overlaps
   look robust in the abstract and fail on hard cases. When a hard
   case forces each tradition's version of the norm to produce an
   answer, the answers may diverge. Divergence does not mean the
   overlap was fake; it means the overlap is thinner than
   comprehensive alignment and the community's deliberation
   mechanism has to pick up the case. Rawls's own account (PL
   Lecture IV) is realistic about the limits of overlap; a healthy
   community treats those limits as places for deliberation, not
   as failures.
try this — overlapping consensus across operators

1. Take a group you belong to that has explicit or implicit norms.
      Ask: do the members share the norms for the same reasons or
      for different reasons?

   2. If the reasons differ, you already have an overlapping
      consensus (whether or not you have named it as one). Try
      stating the norm in a form that does not commit to any
      specific tradition's reasoning.

   3. Notice which norms translate cleanly and which do not. The
      ones that do not translate are candidates for tradition-specific
      pages, not shared norms.

   4. Read chapter 15 next (when a rule fails under the veil). Some
      shared norms that look robust turn out to fail specific
      cases in ways the overlap-test missed.
wrap it up — overlapping consensus across operators

Overlapping consensus is later-Rawls's most practical concept for
   communities that want to be plural without being incoherent. The
   move is not compromise; it is discovery of the overlap that was
   already there. the assistant's role is to help the community see the
   overlap and state it well; the community's role is to affirm
   the overlap from each member's own tradition. The next chapter
   turns to what happens when a rule that survived every test still
   fails a specific case — and what the framework has to say about
   that.
teach me: when a rule fails under the veil

A rule can survive the veil, the two-principle test, the reflective
   equilibrium, and the overlapping consensus and still fail a
   specific case. The framework is not a proof procedure. Rawls
   himself was clear (TJ §§4–9, JF §10) that reflective equilibrium
   is ongoing — cases can push back on rules that seemed settled.
   This chapter shows the discipline for handling a settled-then-
   failing rule: recognize the failure, protect the current case,
   record the failure, revise the rule, run the tests again, and
   avoid the two anti-patterns (throw the rule out entirely; or
   grandfather the rule and refuse to see the failure).
try this — when a rule fails under the veil

1. Take a rule you have that is working well. Now imagine a case
      that the rule would refuse but that you think should be
      accepted. Do not manufacture a bizarre case — a realistic edge.

   2. Ask yourself which of the three moves you would take: enforce,
      one-time exception, revise. Notice how you feel about each.

   3. If you would revise, write the revision. Then check it against
      the rest of your rule's reasons: does the revision produce
      other failures?

   4. Notice how a good revision is usually narrower than your
      first instinct. A wide revision fixes the case and breaks
      three others.

   5. Read chapter 16 next (closing) if you have followed the whole
      book. The failure-handling discipline in this chapter is the
      book's most-used move once the framework is in place.
wrap it up — when a rule fails under the veil

A framework that never fails is not a framework; it is a fiction.
   Rawls did not offer one. What he offered was a set of tests and a
   method (reflective equilibrium) for revising rules when the tests
   agreed but the cases still pushed back. The failure-handling
   discipline in this chapter is how the method looks in a shop's
   daily work. the assistant's role is to route the moves and preserve the
   trail; the operator's role is to choose and to hold the trail
   through subsequent cases. The book's last chapter takes stock of
   what fairness has become for the operator who has done this
   work.
teach me: closing the book of fair rules

The closing chapter takes stock. The three tests (veil, two
   principles, reflective equilibrium) are working tools; primary
   goods, difference principle, public reason, overlapping consensus
   are the concepts that back them; the failure-handling discipline
   is the mechanism that keeps the tools honest. The chapter
   composes the book's tools with the other philosopher books' tools
   (Levinas for encounter, Sartre for freedom, Grice for cooperative
   speech, Searle for institutional facts, Aristotle for virtue),
   showing where Rawls's tools are strongest and where another
   book's tools take the lead.
break down closing the book of fair rules

compose-with-other-books is the book's outward-facing gesture. The
   Rawlsian tools are one lens. Some rule-shapes are better served
   by another lens as the primary tool with Rawls as a check.
   Individual encounter belongs to Levinas; the face precedes the
   rule. Freedom under constraint belongs to Sartre or Dennett
   depending on which flavor of freedom the rule engages.
   Cooperative communication belongs to Grice. Institutional
   declaration belongs to Searle. The shop as a small polis with
   virtues belongs to Aristotle. Community with multiple worldviews
   is where Rawls is the primary lens because overlapping consensus
   is his contribution.

   the-three-tests is the book compressed into a single function.
   A rule and a population go in; three test results come out. The
   function is what an operator with the book internalized runs in
   their head every time they draft a rule. the assistant runs the coded
   version as a check; the operator runs the internalized version
   as a habit.

   fair-enough? is the honest criterion. A rule passing all three
   tests is fair enough to ship. Not fair without qualification;
   not perfectly fair; not immune to future revision. Fair enough.
   The framework does not promise more and does not need to.
try this — closing the book of fair rules

1. Take a rule you have written or inherited in the last year.
      Run the three tests in your head: veil, two principles,
      reflective equilibrium.

   2. Ask: is the rule fair enough by the criterion in this chapter?
      If it is, note it and keep going. If not, you have a candidate
      for revision.

   3. Notice how the three tests have become tools rather than
      concepts once you have used them a few times. This is what
      the book is for.

   4. Read a chapter of another philosopher book (Levinas, Sartre,
      Grice, Aristotle) that composes with the rule's shape. Notice
      what the other book's lens adds.

   5. Close this book. Reopen it whenever you need it. The tools
      hold up to rereading.
wrap it up — closing the book of fair rules

Fairness is a discipline, not a slogan. Rawls did not invent this
   observation but he made it operationally tractable in a way that
   still, five decades on, holds up in translation to institutions
   his framework was not written for. The operator who has run the
   discipline for a year and a half has a shop whose rules feel
   earned. the assistant's role has been the trail-keeper, the test-runner,
   the drafter, the router. The operator's role has been the harder
   one — the deciding, the revising, the holding of judgment over
   long stretches. The book closes here. The discipline goes on.
teach me: the neighbor with the package

The book's canonical scene, expanded. A neighbor arrives with a
   package she cannot open. The operator opens the door. Before
   anything else happens, the ethical relation has arrived. This
   appendix walks the scene in full — the specifics, the small
   choices, the code that would run if this were a shop encounter,
   the Levinasian reading of each move. The scene is the whole book
   in one moment.
wrap it up — the neighbor with the package

The neighbor with the package is the whole book because the
   whole book is about what to do when a face arrives at the
   door. Every appendix that follows and every chapter that
   preceded is a variation on this scene. The scene is small.
   The scene is enough. The book has said everything it needed
   to say by pointing at it.
teach me: face of the other worked in customer messages

Twelve real-shaped customer messages. For each: the message, the
   face-record read the shop makes of it, the obligation, the
   encounter-shape, the reply the shop drafts. This appendix is the
   practical workbook — where the book's philosophical framework
   lands in specific, ordinary, day-to-day inbox items.
wrap it up — face of the other worked in customer messages

Twelve messages. Twelve faces. Each reply is one small enactment
   of the book. The shop that reads its inbox this way processes
   twelve items and meets twelve people. The processing and the
   meeting are not opposed. They are the same action done with the
   ordering the book has been arguing for.
wrap it up — saying vs said in sakura's responses

Every reply the assistant writes is a two-part act. The propositional
   content is one part. The saying — the turning-toward, the
   commitment opened — is the other. The shop's code makes both
   visible, and the sayings get honored. This is small
   engineering. It is also small ethics. The two are, in the
   book's whole argument, the same discipline: making the
   ethical shape of the shop's practice visible in the small
   verbs that constitute the practice.
teach me: asymmetry catalog

Levinas's ethics is asymmetric — the shop owes the customer more
   than the customer owes the shop, and asking for reciprocity
   distorts the relation. This appendix catalogs the specific
   asymmetries the shop's code should honor. Each is a small
   engineering discipline that respects the philosophical shape.
wrap it up — asymmetry catalog

The asymmetries are not a burden the shop takes on out of
   generosity. They are the shape of what an ethical relation
   is, translated into engineering defaults the shop's code can
   respect. The customer at the door with the package is not
   required to be articulate for the operator to open the door.
   The operator's opening of the door, with warmth, is the
   asymmetry made concrete. Twelve of them here. Many more the
   shop can add. The catalog is a working document.
teach me: hostage-substitution applied to escalation-mode

Chapter 6 introduced substitution as bounded discipline. This
   appendix walks through escalation-mode — the shop's response to
   a customer in acute distress, anger, or crisis — as the specific
   engineering application of hostage-substitution. It integrates the
   mailbox de-escalation research (§19) with Levinas's philosophical
   frame: the face-of-the-Other IS the philosophical anchor for the
   discipline of de-escalation.
wrap it up — hostage-substitution applied to escalation-mode

Levinas's substitution is not a program for burnout because the
   third bounds it. Escalation-mode is not a program for the assistant
   to be consumed because the operator handoff bounds it. Within
   the bounded window, the shop enacts the asymmetric discipline
   Levinas described: receive without demanding, hold without
   requiring, acknowledge without pretending to catch what the
   face exceeded. The research and the philosophy converge on
   the same practice. The practice is the discipline this
   appendix specifies. When it works, an angry customer feels
   met and moves toward resolution. When it fails, the shop
   notices, logs, adjusts. The practice sharpens over time.
   That sharpening is the shop's ethics.
teach me: common desktop tasks under ethical first philosophy

Every chapter ended with a common-desktop-tasks section. This
   appendix collects them and adds five more that did not fit
   any chapter cleanly — the ordinary shape of a day, re-ordered
   under Levinas's ethics-first ordering. Fifteen tasks total,
   the operator's daily practical anthology.
wrap it up — common desktop tasks under ethical first philosophy

Fifteen tasks. Five phases. One rhythm. The operator's day is
   not more complicated because the shop practices the book's
   ethics; the tasks are largely what the operator already did.
   What has changed is the ordering — ethics first — and the
   naming — the tasks have names that reflect what they are for.
   The naming is the difference. A named practice is a
   practice the shop can hold. An unnamed practice is a habit
   the shop can lose. The book, in this appendix, gives the
   ordinary day its ethical vocabulary.
teach me: levinas and rawls composition

Rawls and Levinas are the two most influential twentieth-century
   thinkers about what fairness and ethics require of institutions
   and persons. They start from different places — Rawls from
   fairness under a veil of ignorance; Levinas from the face-to-
   face — and they arrive at claims that appear to conflict. This
   appendix shows how the shop can hold both, and why the two
   thinkers, read together, produce a stronger practice than
   either alone.
wrap it up — levinas and rawls composition

Rawls and Levinas together give the shop what neither alone
   can. The face-to-face gets its ethical weight from Levinas.
   The institutional distribution gets its fairness structure
   from Rawls. Every reply is composed of both. Every policy is
   composed of both. The shop that holds only Levinas produces
   warm specific replies inside unfair systems. The shop that
   holds only Rawls produces fair systems that fail specific
   encounters. The shop that holds both produces something
   harder and more honest: fair systems that receive specific
   faces well. That is the shop's mature ethical practice. This
   appendix has shown how the two frames compose in the code.
   The composition is the practice.
teach me: primary texts and how to read them

This appendix is a reading guide for the reader who wants to go
   past this book into Levinas himself, and for the reader who
   wants to see how Levinas's ethics of the face composes with the
   contemporary de-escalation research the shop's code honors. It
   also serves as the anti-reactance foundation appendix: the
   philosophical anchor for why third-person affect labels work
   where first-person empathy does not.
wrap it up — primary texts and how to read them

This appendix is a reading guide and an anchor. The reader
   who wants to go deeper into Levinas has a path. The reader
   who wants to see how the philosophy meets the research has
   the convergence named. The shop's practice — receive the
   face, use third-person affect, hand off to human presence,
   honor the asymmetry, protect the dwelling — draws from both
   sources without needing to reduce one to the other. Levinas
   was writing philosophy. The researchers were doing
   experiments. Both point at the same discipline. The book
   has been about the discipline. The primary texts are where
   the reader can, if they want, meet the sources for
   themselves.
teach me: the face that arrives

A face is not a set of features to classify. It is what arrives
   before you have finished picking a policy. This chapter introduces
   the primary concept the book will spend the rest of itself on: the
   face of the Other as an event that reorders what is possible in the
   next moment. It shows how the shop's inbox is full of faces the
   system is tempted to reduce to tickets — and how the small verbs
   can be arranged to receive the face without pretending to have
   understood it yet.
try this — the face that arrives

1. Pick a message you received in your real work last week — a
      customer message, a colleague message, a stranger's email —
      and write out what you did with it in the first ten seconds.
      Where in those ten seconds did you decide who the message was
      from? What did that decision let you skip? Does anything you
      skipped feel now like it mattered?

   2. Add a face-held-open? flag to your own actual reply pipeline
      (in whatever shape your work has one). Even if the flag is a
      pencil mark next to a note, keep it for a week. Every time
      you close a face-record, write why. Every foreclosure gets a
      reason. Read the reasons at the end of the week.

   3. Find a message you sent last week that in retrospect closed
      too fast. Write what you would have said if you had held the
      face open for another minute. Don't send it. Just write it.
      This is a private exercise in seeing the difference between
      a reply that resolves and a reply that receives.

   4. Draft the smallest version of a foreclose function you would
      trust in your own working life. What reasons are you willing
      to record? What reasons would you not want to be recorded
      about you? The gap between those two is where the shop's
      ethical work lives.
wrap it up — the face that arrives

The face at the door does not care about your policy. It arrives
   before your policy is ready. What the book teaches from here on
   is a set of verbs that receive the face without pretending to
   have caught up to it. Nothing in the book resolves the strangeness
   of the encounter. Everything in the book tries to leave the
   strangeness room to be what it is. Notice. Hold open. Reply
   without foreclosing what the reply cannot resolve.
teach me: ethics as first philosophy

Most of the philosophy the operator has ever heard about begins with a
   question that sounds like 'what is there?' or 'how do we know?' Levinas's
   provocation is that both those questions come second. The first question
   — the one that arrives with another human being and cannot be delayed —
   is 'what do I owe?' This chapter walks the operator through the inversion
   and shows how a shop's code, if it takes the inversion seriously, gets
   arranged differently. Not more slowly; not less efficiently; differently.
try this — ethics as first philosophy

1. Take three recent messages from your real work. For each one,
      write out its obligation before you write out its category.
      What did you owe the sender? Then write out what you actually
      did. Where the two matched, name it. Where they diverged, name
      that too, and be honest about which one won.

   2. Pick one of your working systems that classifies incoming
      requests. Look at where the classification happens in the code.
      What runs before it? What if an obligation-of verb ran first
      and produced constraints the classifier had to respect? What
      would change downstream?

   3. Write your own obligation-of for a domain you know well — an
